Environmental and postnatal factors further modulate this risk, with infections and stress triggering both initial symptom onset and exacerbations in nearly 88% of affected children, while approximately 80% experience loss of developmental skills. The underlying mechanisms involve genetic factors regulating synaptic formation, transcriptional regulation, and chromatin-remodeling pathways, processes refined through microglial-mediated synaptic pruning that can be dysregulated by prenatal inflammation. Clinical data from the NDD-ECHO cohort demonstrates that neurodevelopmental disorders frequently co-occur with asthma, allergic disorders, and autoimmune conditions, with 28% of children presenting with PANS phenotype characterized by infection-triggered abrupt-onset obsessive-compulsive symptoms. Treatment approaches include conventional pharmacotherapy with clonidine, methylphenidate, and fluoxetine, alongside emerging immune-modulating therapies such as intravenous immunoglobulin and azithromycin, reflecting the dual contribution of genetic susceptibility and immune-inflammatory mechanisms in these disorders.

title: Obesity and asthma during pregnancy: a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review and meta-analysis demonstrates that obesity and asthma are interconnected risk factors that substantially compromise maternal and fetal health during pregnancy. Obesity increases the risk of asthma development (OR 2.42) and uncontrolled asthma (OR 1.29) in pregnant women, while concurrent asthma and obesity elevates the likelihood of gestational diabetes mellitus (OR 4.35) and caesarean delivery more than either condition alone. Asthma during pregnancy is independently associated with multiple adverse outcomes including hypertensive disorders, gestational diabetes, fetal growth restriction, preterm birth, and severe maternal morbidity. The biological mechanisms underlying these associations involve obesity-induced systemic inflammation through adipose tissue production of pro-inflammatory cytokines and leptin, which promotes airway hyperactivity and altered patterns of airway inflammation (elevated neutrophils, eosinophils, and interleukin-5). The combination of asthma and obesity creates a particularly dangerous phenotype characterized by poor asthma control and diminished responsiveness to conventional therapies, highlighting the critical need for early obesity management and careful asthma monitoring before and during pregnancy to mitigate these serious perinatal complications.

title: First–Second-Trimester Dietary Inflammatory Index and Anemia Risk in the Third Trimester: A Prospective Cohort Study
summary:
# Summary

This prospective cohort study demonstrates that pro-inflammatory dietary patterns during pregnancy, quantified by the Dietary Inflammatory Index (DII), significantly increase the risk of third-trimester anemia in a dose-dependent manner. A one-unit increase in first- or second-trimester DII scores corresponded to 62.3% and 41.3% higher anemia risk, respectively, with pro-inflammatory diet groups exhibiting a 3.73-fold elevated risk. The mechanistic pathway involves pro-inflammatory diets triggering systemic elevation of inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, CRP), which stimulate hepatic hepcidin synthesis, leading to iron malabsorption through ferritin degradation and reduced erythrocyte lifespan via oxidative stress and eryptosis. Third-trimester anemia itself is strongly associated with adverse pregnancy outcomes including postpartum hemorrhage and preterm birth. Iron supplementation serves as a critical preventive strategy, though anti-inflammatory dietary interventions incorporating whole grains, dark leafy vegetables, and antioxidant-rich foods offer complementary protective effects through DII reduction.

title: Reproductive-associated risk factors and incident coronary heart disease in women: an umbrella review
journal: American Heart Journal Plus: Cardiology Research and Practice
summary:
# Summary

This umbrella review establishes that multiple reproductive-associated risk factors significantly elevate women's coronary heart disease and cardiovascular disease risk. Adverse pregnancy outcomes—particularly preeclampsia (RR 2.5), gestational diabetes mellitus (RR 1.98–2.31), preterm birth (HR 1.38), and stillbirth (RR 1.41–1.60)—demonstrate strong associations with incident coronary heart disease and ischemic heart disease, with elevated risk persisting years postpartum. Reproductive conditions including polycystic ovary syndrome (pooled RR 1.51), premature ovarian insufficiency (HR 1.69), and recurrent miscarriage (RR 1.37) also substantially increase cardiovascular risk. The evidence indicates that hypertensive disorders of pregnancy pose the highest immediate CVD risk in the first decade postpartum, while the additive effects of multiple reproductive factors further compound coronary heart disease susceptibility. Integrating these reproductive risk factors into cardiovascular risk prediction models and screening tools may enable earlier identification and intervention in women with adverse reproductive histories.

title: A thinner endometrium is associated with a greater risk of preterm delivery after fresh cleavage embryo transfer but not after blastocyst transfer: a retrospective cohort study of 11,111 singleton live births
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective cohort study of 11,111 singleton live births demonstrates that endometrial thickness (EMT) measured on the day of hCG trigger is a critical predictor of preterm delivery risk following assisted reproductive technology, with important differences between embryo transfer types. Thin endometrium (EMT <7.5 mm) significantly increases preterm delivery risk in patients undergoing cleavage-stage embryo transfer, approximately doubling or tripling the odds compared to those with optimal thickness (7.5–12 mm), and is also associated with low birth weight, small for gestational age, and increased cesarean delivery rates. Conversely, thicker endometrium (EMT ≥12 mm) provides protective effects, reducing preterm delivery risk by 23–37% across both transfer types after adjustment for confounding factors. Notably, the protective association of thick endometrium against preterm delivery persists in blastocyst transfer cycles, whereas the detrimental effect of thin endometrium is less pronounced in this population, suggesting that embryo stage and endometrial quality interact to influence pregnancy outcomes. The study confirms that endometrial thickness serves as an independent indicator of endometrial receptivity and subsequent placental development, with thin endometrium linked to multiple adverse perinatal outcomes including placental disorders, growth restriction, and hypertension-related complications.

title: Causal relationships between metabolic syndrome, plasma metabolites, and female reproductive diseases: insights from a two-step mendelian randomization approach
summary: Using a two-step Mendelian randomization approach, this study establishes metabolic syndrome as a causal driver of multiple female reproductive and pregnancy complications, including polycystic ovary syndrome, uterine fibroids, gestational diabetes mellitus, eclampsia, and miscarriage. Metabolic syndrome disrupts hormonal homeostasis and alters plasma metabolite profiles, with specific metabolic signatures identified for each disease: elevated glucose and lysophosphatidylcholine for gestational diabetes; triglycerides in HDL particles and ketone bodies for polycystic ovary syndrome; and amino acids including alanine and phenylalanine for eclampsia. Protective lipid metabolites, particularly cholesterol esters and total cholesterol in large HDL particles, were found to reduce eclampsia risk, while small HDL particle components increased risk. The analysis also reveals that genetically predicted obesity and visceral adipose tissue mass causally influence reproductive diseases, establishing metabolic dysfunction as a unifying pathogenic mechanism across endometriosis-related conditions and adverse pregnancy outcomes. These findings support targeting metabolic abnormalities and specific plasma metabolites as potential intervention strategies for improving female reproductive health outcomes.

title: Congenital anomalies observed in children conceived through assisted reproductive technology—a systematic review and meta-analysis
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary: This meta-analysis of 70 studies reveals that children conceived through assisted reproductive technology (ART) have a marginally elevated risk of congenital anomalies compared to naturally conceived controls, though the magnitude of risk varies by specific ART methodology. Subgroup analyses identified particular procedural factors associated with higher anomaly rates: intracytoplasmic sperm injection (ICSI), Day 3 embryo transfer, and fresh embryo transfer showed increased congenital anomaly risk, whereas IVF with Day 5 transfer and frozen embryo transfer demonstrated lower risk profiles. The analysis identified congenital heart defects, chromosomal abnormalities (including Down, Edwards, Turner, DiGeorge, and Patau syndromes), and neural tube defects as major contributors to perinatal complications, with heart defects occurring in 0.76% of live births but rising to 1.5% in stillbirths. Critically, the observed association between ART and congenital anomalies may be confounded by underlying parental factors including advanced age, elevated BMI, infertility status, and genetic predisposition rather than ART procedures themselves. Additional risk factors such as ovulation induction and hormone administration showed weaker associations, and temporal analysis revealed consistent findings across pre-2015 and post-2015 studies, suggesting the relationship between ART and congenital anomalies remains stable over time.

title: A Novel Approach for Self‐Care in Pregnant Women With Hypothyroidism: Mobile‐Based Development and Insights
summary:
# Summary

Maternal hypothyroidism during pregnancy represents a significant clinical concern, characterized by decreased thyroid hormone production resulting in low free thyroxine (FT4) and elevated thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH). This condition substantially increases the risk of multiple adverse pregnancy outcomes, including gestational hypertension, preeclampsia, gestational diabetes, and preterm labor, while also impairing fetal neural and cognitive development and elevating the risk of intellectual developmental delays and low birth weight. To address this clinical challenge, researchers developed a comprehensive mobile-based self-care application for pregnant women with hypothyroidism containing 58 informative elements and 9 vital capabilities, including medical information, laboratory test tracking, medication management, nutrition guidance, lifestyle recommendations, and reminders for appointments and medication intake. Mobile-based health technologies enhance patient awareness and self-management capacity while improving health behaviors and reducing disease complications through increased healthcare provider-patient interaction speed and patient involvement in disease management. The integration of such digital self-care tools with prenatal supplementation containing essential nutrients like iron, folic acid, and DHA, along with pre-pregnancy thyroid screening to enable timely treatment, represents a holistic approach to optimizing maternal and fetal outcomes in hypothyroid pregnancies.

title: A comparative systematic review and meta-analysis of uterine artery resistance in pregnant women with and without previous history of cesarean section
summary:
# Summary

A systematic review and meta-analysis demonstrates that women with a history of cesarean section exhibit significantly increased uterine artery resistance during subsequent pregnancies, measured primarily through the Pulsatility Index. This elevation in vascular resistance is mechanistically linked to cesarean-induced uterine scarring, which impairs normal vascular remodeling, induces localized fibrosis and ischemia, and compromises trophoblastic invasion and spiral artery remodeling. Increased uterine artery resistance and elevated Pulsatility Index are associated with multiple adverse pregnancy outcomes, including preeclampsia, intrauterine growth restriction, small-for-gestational-age fetuses, and neonatal complications such as low birth weight and respiratory distress. The findings suggest that prior cesarean delivery creates long-term hemodynamic alterations that increase the risk of placental insufficiency and related maternal-fetal complications in future pregnancies. These results support the clinical utility of monitoring uterine artery resistance in pregnant women with previous cesarean sections to identify those at heightened risk for adverse outcomes.

title: Is there an association between lateral uterine localization of the placenta and pregnancy outcomes?
summary: This study investigates whether lateral placental positioning affects pregnancy outcomes. The research found that lateral placentation is associated with a prolonged third stage of labor and increased retained placenta rates, with multivariate analysis confirming lateral placentation as an independent predictor of longer labor duration. Notably, lateral placentation was not significantly associated with major adverse outcomes including hypertensive disorders, small for gestational age, or preterm birth in this cohort. However, previous meta-analyses have linked lateral placental location to increased risks of preeclampsia, hypertensive disorders, preterm birth, and fetal growth restriction, suggesting that impaired placental perfusion from reduced uterine blood flow may be a mechanistic pathway. The study also observed that lateral placentation correlated with advanced maternal age and lower nulliparity rates, and was associated with reduced cesarean delivery rates. These findings suggest that while lateral placentation has demonstrable effects on labor progression and placental delivery, its broader clinical significance for adverse pregnancy outcomes remains somewhat inconsistent across studies.

title: Spontaneous haemoperitoneum in pregnancy complicated by a pulmonary embolus: a case report
summary: This case report describes a rare but serious obstetric complication where a patient with stage 4 endometriosis developed spontaneous haemoperitoneum during pregnancy, subsequently complicated by pulmonary embolism. Endometriosis and assisted reproductive technology are well-established risk factors for spontaneous haemoperitoneum in pregnancy, with the condition occurring in approximately 1 in 25,000 pregnancies and associated with endometriosis in over 55% of reported cases. The pathophysiology involves chronic inflammation, vascular proliferation, and decidualization of ectopic endometrial tissue, which predisposes to bleeding. Management required conservative treatment with tranexamic acid and analgesia for the haemoperitoneum, followed by anticoagulation with enoxaparin after pulmonary embolism developed, with the additional complication of postpartum haemorrhage requiring blood transfusion. Multiple maternal risk factors—recent pregnancy, haemorrhage, immobility, and thrombophilia—contributed to the venous thromboembolism, highlighting the need for careful thromboprophylaxis in this high-risk population. The case underscores the importance of considering spontaneous haemoperitoneum in pregnant patients with endometriosis presenting with acute abdominal pain or hemodynamic instability.

title: Clomiphene Citrate Medication for Infertility and Risk of Stillbirth or Neonatal Death: A Population-based Cohort Study
journal: The Journal of Clinical Endocrinology and Metabolism
summary: This population-based cohort study found that clomiphene citrate (CC) exposure for infertility treatment is associated with increased risk of perinatal death, with a combined odds ratio of 1.54 for stillbirth and neonatal death in singleton pregnancies. Among women treated with CC, the prevalence of stillbirth was 10.2 per 1,000 births and neonatal death was 3.1 per 1,000, compared to lower rates in unexposed pregnancies. The elevated perinatal mortality risk persists after adjustment for confounding factors including obesity, pregestational diabetes, maternal age, smoking, and socioeconomic status. Mechanistically, CC's adverse effects may operate through multiple pathways: its selective estrogen receptor modulation causing reduced endometrial thickening, bioaccumulation of CC and its metabolites in maternal tissues, epigenetic changes, and DNA strand breaks that could impair placental and fetal development. The study also highlights that women receiving CC often have underlying conditions like polycystic ovary syndrome, which itself is associated with endometrial dysfunction and increased stillbirth risk through metabolic complications including type 2 diabetes and hypertension. These findings support the recent clinical shift toward letrozole as first-line ovulation induction therapy in women with polycystic ovary syndrome, particularly given the documented biological plausibility of adverse outcomes with CC exposure.

title: Natural cycle versus hormone replacement therapy as endometrial preparation in ovulatory women undergoing frozen-thawed embryo transfer: The COMPETE open-label randomized controlled trial
summary: The COMPETE trial demonstrates that natural cycle (NC) endometrial preparation for frozen-thawed embryo transfer in ovulatory women achieves superior reproductive outcomes compared to hormone replacement therapy (HRT), with NC yielding a 54.0% live birth rate versus 43.0% for HRT. This advantage is mechanistically linked to thicker endometrium in NC cycles and the maintenance of physiological hormone levels, as HRT causes aberrant estradiol and progesterone levels that damage the endometrium, disrupt the window of implantation, and impair endometrial receptivity. NC cycles also demonstrated significantly lower miscarriage rates (RR 0.61) and antepartum hemorrhage rates (RR 0.63), outcomes attributed to the corpus luteum's natural production of vascular endothelial growth factor and relaxin, which support healthy vascular adaptation and placental development. In contrast, HRT's unphysiological hormone profile increases risk of obstetric complications including placental abnormalities and compromised fetoplacental blood flow. The findings support NC as the preferred endometrial preparation strategy in women with regular menstrual cycles, as it provides physiologically optimal conditions for implantation and pregnancy maintenance while avoiding the adverse effects associated with exogenous hormone administration.

title: Associations of Nausea and Vomiting of Pregnancy with Maternal and Fetal Outcomes
summary:
# Summary

Nausea and vomiting in pregnancy (NVP) are common conditions associated with multiple maternal and fetal outcomes, driven primarily by placental production of growth differentiation factor 15 (GDF15), which directly induces appetite loss, taste aversion, nausea, vomiting, and weight loss. Risk factors for NVP include young maternal age, female fetal sex, multiple pregnancy, elevated human chorionic gonadotropin, and pre-existing gastrointestinal, endocrine, or thyroid disorders, with genetic predisposition evident through GDF15 gene variations linked to hyperemesis gravidarum. Paradoxically, while NVP is associated with gestational hypertension and metabolic complications including hyponatremia, hypokalemia, and ketonuria, women experiencing NVP showed reduced rates of gestational diabetes and cesarean delivery, possibly due to enhanced insulin sensitivity from reduced caloric intake and lower gestational weight gain. More severe manifestations such as hyperemesis gravidarum are associated with adverse neonatal outcomes including preterm birth and require interventions including intravenous fluid administration, thiamine supplementation, and antiemetic medications such as doxylamine succinate/pyridoxine hydrochloride, metoclopramide, ondansetron, and prednisolone.

title: Incidence, outcomes and management of spontaneous haemoperitoneum in pregnancy: a UK population-based study
summary: This UK population-based study reveals that spontaneous haemoperitoneum in pregnancy (SHiP), though rare, represents a serious obstetric emergency with severe maternal and fetal consequences. The condition is strongly associated with preterm birth, high perinatal mortality, and maternal mortality, with identified risk factors including maternal age over 35 years, endometriosis, artificial reproductive techniques, and multiple pregnancy. The most common bleeding sources are splenic, hepatic, or renal aneurysms and parenchymal ruptures (45% of cases), though extrauterine endometrial implants and pelvic varicose veins can also contribute. Patients typically present with acute abdominal pain, hypovolaemic shock, decreased haemoglobin, and fetal distress; diagnosis relies on ultrasound and computed tomography detection of free peritoneal fluid. Management is predominantly surgical, involving laparotomy and caesarean section in the majority of cases, supported by haemostatic medications including tranexamic acid, factor VIII, and fibrinogen. The condition carries substantial morbidity, with 60% of women requiring intensive care and 30% experiencing major complications such as renal failure, disseminated intravascular coagulation, and multiorgan failure, while affected infants face preterm complications including respiratory distress syndrome, hypoxia, and hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy.

title: Recurrence Risk of Pregnancy Complications in Twin and Singleton Deliveries
summary:
# Summary

This study examined pregnancy complication recurrence risks across different delivery plurality patterns, finding that maternal factors including race/ethnicity, age, weight, and gestational weight gain strongly associate with adverse outcomes including preterm birth, gestational diabetes mellitus, gestational hypertension, and preeclampsia/eclampsia. Multiple gestations significantly increase the risk of preterm birth, gestational diabetes, hypertensive disorders, and puerperal endometriosis compared to singleton pregnancies. Recurrence risks vary substantially by pregnancy plurality pattern, with singleton-singleton subsequent pregnancies showing the highest relative risk for preterm birth (aRR=5.1) and gestational diabetes (aRR=22.7), while twin-singleton pregnancies demonstrate the highest recurrence for gestational hypertension (aRR=7.6) and preeclampsia/eclampsia (aRR=9.2). Previous preterm birth in twin pregnancies confers a four-fold increased risk of preterm birth in subsequent singleton pregnancies. Notably, a prior pregnancy of multiples paradoxically reduces recurrence risk for gestational hypertension and preeclampsia/eclampsia in subsequent pregnancies, suggesting multiplicity-related physiological factors may influence long-term hypertensive risk profiles.

title: Impact of climate change and environmental adversities on maternal and fetal health: the role of clinical practices and providers in mitigating effects and prioritising women's health in the UK
summary:
# Summary

This article examines how climate change and environmental adversities threaten maternal and fetal health through multiple interconnected pathways. Environmental stressors including extreme weather events, heat exposure, water contamination, and air pollution directly increase risks of adverse pregnancy outcomes such as preterm birth, low birth weight, miscarriage, and stillbirth, while also elevating maternal stress and causing nutritional deficiencies that compound these risks. Chemical contaminants in water—particularly BPA, phthalates, and PFAS—are associated with both adverse obstetric outcomes and endometriosis, highlighting the link between environmental pollutants and reproductive health conditions. The article emphasizes that clinical providers can mitigate these effects through evidence-based practices, such as replacing high-carbon anaesthetic gases and disposable materials with sustainable alternatives, transitioning healthcare facilities to renewable energy, and ensuring adequate prenatal care to address climate-related vulnerabilities in pregnant women.

title: Adenomyomectomy with preconceptional transabdominal cervicoisthmic cerclage for transvaginal cerclage failure
summary: Adenomyosis, characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue in the myometrium, is strongly associated with multiple adverse pregnancy outcomes including infertility, preterm birth, premature rupture of membranes, preeclampsia, placental abnormalities, and postpartum hemorrhage. Women with adenomyosis who experience transvaginal cerclage failure represent a particularly high-risk population requiring innovative surgical approaches. Adenomyomectomy combined with preconceptional transabdominal cervicoisthmic cerclage (TCIC) emerges as a promising uterus-conserving treatment strategy for these patients, offering improved pregnancy outcomes and delivery after 34 weeks of gestation. This combined surgical intervention addresses both the underlying adenomyotic pathology and cervical insufficiency, reducing surgical complexity and obstetric risks compared to performing TCIC during pregnancy. The approach demonstrates potential to prevent preterm birth and treat second-trimester miscarriage in women with adenomyosis who have failed conventional transvaginal cerclage. These findings suggest that adenomyomectomy with preconceptional TCIC may provide a viable fertility-preserving option for adenomyotic women seeking pregnancy despite previous cerclage failure.

title: Association between pre-pregnancy uterine volume and preterm birth in women with adenomyosis: a retrospective cohort study
summary:
# Summary

Adenomyosis, characterized by endometrial tissue invading the myometrium and causing smooth muscle cell hyperplasia and hypertrophy, is strongly associated with multiple adverse pregnancy outcomes including preterm birth, preeclampsia, small-for-gestational-age infants, and increased miscarriage rates. This retrospective cohort study identified pre-pregnancy uterine volume as an independent risk factor for preterm birth in women with adenomyosis, with a median uterine volume of 108.9 cm³ in the preterm birth group compared to the term birth group. The study demonstrated a non-linear relationship between uterine volume and preterm birth risk, with both volumes below 89.34 cm³ and those at or above this threshold showing associations with preterm delivery. Women with focal adenomyosis exhibited stronger associations between uterine volume and preterm birth compared to those with diffuse adenomyosis, suggesting that lesion type influences the mechanism of adverse outcomes, potentially through discordant myometrial contractions that impair placental implantation and increase complications such as placental malposition and preterm premature rupture of membranes.

title: Optimizing Pregnancy Outcomes: The Role of Gynecologists in Preconceptional Care in Italy
summary:
# Summary

Preconceptional nutritional optimization significantly impacts pregnancy outcomes and fertility, with gynecologists playing a critical role in counseling and supplementation strategies. Folic acid supplementation, particularly in the form of 5-methyltetrahydrofolate (5-MTHF) rather than unmetabolized folic acid, reduces neural tube defects and is most effective when combined with vitamin B12, which supports DNA synthesis, methylation processes, and neurodevelopment in offspring. Vitamin D deficiency, common in pregnant women, is linked to preeclampsia, gestational diabetes, and reduced fertility, with adequate levels associated with significantly higher clinical pregnancy and live birth rates, especially in women with vitamin D receptors expressed in reproductive organs. Micronutrient deficiencies broadly increase risks of infertility, fetal structural defects, and long-term offspring diseases, while preconceptional vitamin and mineral supplementation reduces preterm delivery and low birth weight. Specific conditions including endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome, and hormonal imbalances compromise fertility and require clinical management. Nutritional education increases supplement adherence and is therefore essential to improving maternal and fetal health outcomes.

title: Asynchronous twin delivery in uterus didelphys with a 12-week interval: case report and literature review
summary: Uterus didelphys is a congenital Müllerian duct malformation resulting from failure of paramesonephric duct fusion during embryonic development, creating two independent uterine cavities with distinct clinical implications. Women with this condition face significantly elevated risks of adverse pregnancy outcomes including preterm birth, miscarriage, intrauterine growth restriction, abnormal fetal positioning, and postpartum hemorrhage, as well as gynecological symptoms such as dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility. This case report documents an exceptional presentation of asynchronous twin delivery with a 12-week interval between deliveries from separate uterine horns, demonstrating that delayed delivery of the second twin can substantially improve perinatal survival by extending gestational age and reducing neonatal complications such as respiratory distress syndrome, sepsis, and necrotizing enterocolitis. However, prolonged delayed delivery increases maternal morbidity through chorioamniotic inflammation, intrauterine hemorrhage, and infection, requiring careful clinical management with prophylactic measures including low molecular weight heparin for thrombosis prevention and uterine contraction agents with hemostatic drugs to prevent postpartum hemorrhage. Diagnosis relies on imaging modalities ranging from standard ultrasound in asymptomatic cases to three-dimensional ultrasound, magnetic resonance imaging, and endoscopy for complex presentations. Clinical suspicion for uterus didelphys should be heightened in women presenting with dyspareunia, dysmenorrhea, infertility, repeated abortion, or preterm birth.

title: Investigating the Frequency of Placenta Previa and the Associated Risk Factors During Pregnancy
summary:
# Summary

Placenta previa, the abnormal implantation of the placenta over the lower uterine segment, is a significant obstetric condition associated with substantial maternal and fetal morbidity. Multiple risk factors increase placenta previa occurrence, including advanced maternal age, multiparity, multigravidity, previous cesarean sections, assisted reproductive techniques, history of abortion or miscarriage, and gynecological diseases such as endometriosis. The condition causes serious hemorrhagic complications including antepartum, intrapartum, and postpartum hemorrhage, with 44.4% of affected women requiring blood transfusion, and generates adverse fetal and neonatal outcomes including fetal asphyxia, low birth weight, intrauterine death, preterm birth, respiratory distress syndrome, and neonatal mortality at 280 per 1000 live births. Patients typically present with painless vaginal bleeding during the second or third trimester, often recurrent, and require cesarean delivery in the majority of cases. A past history of placenta previa is a particularly strong risk factor, with 66.6% of women experiencing recurrence and nine-fold increased risk. The condition represents a significant cause of maternal and perinatal mortality, necessitating careful identification of at-risk populations and appropriate obstetric management.

title: Integrated care for multi-domain vulnerability during pregnancy: a retrospective cohort study
summary: This retrospective cohort study demonstrates that multi-domain vulnerability—encompassing psychiatric illness, social factors, and chronic physical illness—affects 83% of pregnant women and substantially increases risk for adverse maternal and neonatal outcomes including pre-eclampsia, small for gestational age, preterm birth, and infant mortality. The most common psychiatric diagnoses were depressive disorder (22%), anxiety disorder (14.1%), and posttraumatic stress disorder, frequently co-occurring with chronic physical conditions such as obesity (23%), polycystic ovary syndrome, hypothyroidism, and autoimmune diseases, as well as with social stressors including relationship issues (57%), financial hardship (25.6%), and severe housing problems. Nearly half of women experienced unintended pregnancies, while 11.2% reported domestic abuse and 17% were smoking during pregnancy, with substance use identified in 13.1% of the cohort. The study reveals critical intersectionality in vulnerability patterns, where women with psychiatric illness had concurrent social factors in 69% of cases and chronic physical illness in 58.8%, indicating that these risk domains rarely occur in isolation. These findings underscore the necessity for integrated, multi-domain screening and intervention during pregnancy to address the complex interplay between mental health, social determinants, and physical comorbidities that drive adverse pregnancy outcomes.

title: First live birth after in vitro fertilization in a woman with Alström syndrome: a case report
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes the first successful in vitro fertilization pregnancy in a woman with Alström syndrome, a rare genetic disorder caused by mutations in the ALMS1 gene that disrupts ciliary function. The patient presented with multiple metabolic and reproductive complications typical of Alström syndrome, including insulin resistance, hyperandrogenism, oligomenorrhea, endometriosis, and polycystic ovarian changes, which collectively impaired her fertility. Additionally, her male partner exhibited severe oligoasthenoteratozoospermia, necessitating intracytoplasmic sperm injection (ICSI) combined with preimplantation genetic testing for aneuploidies (PGT-A) to achieve conception. During pregnancy, the patient required careful monitoring and management of chronic hypertension, gestational diabetes, and increased preeclampsia risk due to her underlying ciliary dysfunction affecting placental vascular development. The successful pregnancy outcome was achieved through coordinated reproductive and metabolic management, including antihypertensive therapy and acetylsalicylic acid prophylaxis, demonstrating that assisted reproductive techniques can overcome the significant fertility challenges posed by Alström syndrome.

title: Thyroid and female fertility: a survey on the clinical practice among endocrinologists belonging to the italian thyroid association
summary: This survey examines the critical relationship between thyroid function and female fertility among Italian endocrinologists. Thyroid hormones play essential roles in ovulation, embryo implantation, and pregnancy maintenance, while thyroid autoimmunity—affecting approximately 10% of reproductive-age women—is significantly associated with infertility, unexplained infertility, polycystic ovary syndrome, and endometriosis. Thyroid autoimmunity triggers inflammatory infiltration and impairs adequate thyroid hormone production, leading to hypothyroidism or hyperthyroidism, both of which cause menstrual cycle disturbances and increased miscarriage risk. Thyroid disorders have broad detrimental effects on pregnancy outcomes, including recurrent pregnancy loss, preeclampsia, preterm delivery, placental abruption, low birth weight, and neonatal respiratory distress. The survey emphasizes that cytokines and chemokines involved in thyroid autoimmunity contribute to ovarian dysfunction and aging, establishing thyroid health as interconnected with ovarian reserve and function. Clinicians stress the importance of iodine supplementation during preconception planning and appropriate L-thyroxine dosing in hypothyroid women attempting pregnancy, with particular attention to estrogen-induced changes in TSH levels.

title: Extracellular vesicles as prospective biological indicators for midgestational placental complications in the mouse
journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that placental extracellular vesicles (EVs) serve as biological indicators of midgestational placental complications by reflecting the molecular signatures of compromised placental development. Using conditional knockout mice, the researchers showed that Nodal deletion in uterine tissue—a signaling pathway critical for trophoblast differentiation, proliferation, and invasion—causes intrauterine growth restriction, fetal loss, and defects in placentation and angiogenesis. Through bioinformatics analysis of EV cargo from midgestational placentas, the study identified 31 differentially expressed proteins and 10 microRNAs associated with placental development, oxidative stress, angiogenesis, and immunomodulation, with 15 proteins and six miRNAs previously linked to pregnancy complications. The research revealed that pathological placental EVs—including procoagulant EVs from activated endothelial cells and EVs from hypoxic trophoblasts—can impair fetal growth, reduce placental diameter, and suppress angiogenic pathways. These findings establish placental EVs as minimally invasive liquid biomarkers capable of early detection of reproductive complications including preeclampsia, intrauterine growth restriction, and preterm birth, offering potential clinical applications for pregnancy monitoring.

title: Microbial dysbiosis and associated disease mechanisms in maternal and child health
summary:
# Summary

Maternal and infant microbiome dysbiosis represents a critical factor influencing pregnancy outcomes and child health through multiple interconnected mechanisms. The gut, vaginal, and placental microbiomes play fundamental roles in shaping immune development, metabolic control, and brain growth, while dysbiosis triggers inflammation and oxidative stress that compromise these functions. Maternal age, antibiotic exposure, and cesarean delivery promote microbial imbalances that increase risks for preterm birth, gestational diabetes, preeclampsia, and neurodevelopmental disorders in offspring. Dysbiosis-induced overproduction of pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α, IL-1) and conditions such as bacterial vaginosis further disrupt pregnancy and neonatal outcomes, with effects potentially extending to altered neurotransmitter levels and childhood obesity. Therapeutic interventions including probiotics, prebiotics, fecal microbiota transplantation, and precision microbiome therapeutics offer potential strategies to mitigate dysbiosis-associated complications and restore healthy microbiota equilibrium.

title: Predictive Capacity of First Trimester Diagnosis of Placenta Previa
summary: This study examines the predictive value of first trimester transabdominal ultrasound (TAUS) diagnosis for placenta previa persistence and its clinical implications. The authors found that placenta previa diagnosed in the first trimester has poor predictive capacity, with 86% of cases resolving by delivery, indicating limited utility for early detection. Risk factors for placenta previa persistence include advanced maternal age, posterior placental location, and specific sonographic measurements such as placental edge overlap greater than 23 millimeters or extension exceeding 15 millimeters over the internal os. Notably, endometriosis, prior cesarean delivery, and assisted reproductive technology are associated with increased placenta previa risk. The study emphasizes that first trimester TAUS diagnosis may cause unnecessary maternal anxiety and activity restrictions, since most cases will resolve, though even resolved midtrimester previa carries risks for postpartum hemorrhage and uterine atony. These findings suggest that first trimester placenta previa diagnosis should be interpreted cautiously and patients should receive appropriate counseling to mitigate psychological burden.

title: Clinical features and outcomes of peripartum obstetric patients admitted to the intensive care unit: A nationwide inpatient database in Japan
summary:
# Summary

This nationwide Japanese database study analyzed peripartum patients requiring intensive care, identifying hemorrhage as the leading cause of ICU admission (52.6%), followed by hypertensive disorders of pregnancy (16.7%). Hemorrhage-related cases were primarily driven by uterine atony, disseminated intravascular coagulation, placenta previa, placental abruption, and placenta accreta, with most patients receiving blood transfusions (71.5%) and some requiring transcatheter arterial embolization (18.0%). Notably, patients who did not survive had lower proportions of hemorrhage cases but significantly higher prevalence of amniotic fluid embolism, cardiovascular disease, cerebrovascular disease, infection, and pulmonary disease, with intracranial hemorrhage showing the highest mortality rate (20.5%). Risk factors for severe peripartum complications included advanced maternal age, obesity, and use of reproductive technologies, while endometriosis and adenomyosis were rare comorbidities at admission (0.2% each). The findings underscore that while treatable hemorrhage is the most common cause of obstetric ICU admission, non-hemorrhagic complications involving multi-organ dysfunction carry substantially higher mortality risk and require specialized critical care management.

title: Hyperemesis in Pregnancy: Complications and Treatment
summary: Hyperemesis gravidarum (HG) is a severe pregnancy condition characterized by persistent nausea, vomiting, and associated symptoms including excessive salivation, fatigue, weakness, altered taste perception, and mood disturbances, with established risk factors including first-trimester pregnancy, primigravidity, multiple gestation, obesity, and hyperthyroid disorders. The condition triggers a cascade of serious maternal complications spanning metabolic abnormalities (dehydration, electrolyte imbalances, hyponatremia, ketonuria), neurological sequelae (Wernicke's encephalopathy, central pontine myelinolysis, seizures), and thrombotic events (deep vein thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, cardiac arrhythmias), while also causing significant adverse fetal outcomes including fetal growth restriction, preterm birth, and low birth weight. Elevated circulating levels of growth differentiation factor 15 (GDF15) have been implicated as a key pathophysiological mechanism, as this endocrine signal triggers nausea, weight loss, and anorexia through central nervous system signaling. Treatment options include FDA-approved doxylamine-pyridoxine, as well as ondansetron, metoclopramide, and corticosteroids, with non-pharmacological interventions such as acupressure, psychotherapy, and hypnosis offering supplementary benefit. Notably, common reproductive disorders such as endometriosis and polycystic ovary syndrome have not been demonstrated to increase HG risk, suggesting distinct underlying etiopathogenic mechanisms.

title: National natural science foundation of China funding patterns and policy implications for obstetrics and gynecology research: a decade of trends (2011–2020)
journal: Journal of Pharmaceutical Policy and Practice
summary:
# Summary

Between 2011–2020, China's National Natural Science Foundation prioritized research on major gynecological and obstetric conditions, with endometriosis emerging as a frequently studied topic requiring both inflammation-targeting and pharmacological therapeutic solutions. Estrogen-related hormonal therapies and drug management strategies for conditions like gestational diabetes mellitus, hypertensive disorders of pregnancy, and diabetic nephropathy dominated the funding landscape, reflecting the growing clinical burden of these maternal conditions. General Programs, comprising the majority of funding allocations, supported foundational studies on placental angiogenesis and drug responses during pregnancy, while smaller high-level strategic programs focused on maternal-fetal immune tolerance and prenatal diagnostics as drivers of pharmacological discovery. Eastern China institutions demonstrated competitive advantages in maternal-fetal immunology and pharmacogenomics for obstetric care, while central and western institutions addressed regional health priorities such as pregnancy at high altitude and gynecological diseases in ethnic minorities. However, limited interdisciplinary team-based research infrastructure restricted the integration of pharmacogenomic insights into reproductive care and comprehensive investigation of drug safety during pregnancy, highlighting critical gaps in translating basic discoveries into clinical applications.

title: Silent Danger: Risk Factors and Outcomes of Fortuitously Discovered Uterine Rupture – A 41-Case Cohort Study
summary:
# Summary

This 41-case cohort study identifies critical risk factors and clinical presentations of uterine rupture (UR), a rare but life-threatening obstetric complication. Prolonged labor (>220 minutes) emerged as the strongest predictor of complete UR, while incomplete rupture was associated with longer gestational age, extended labor duration, and lower maternal weight. Prior cesarean delivery creates uterine scars that substantially increase rupture risk, though rare cases occur in unscarred uteri with endometriosis, adenomyosis, placenta accreta spectrum, or connective tissue disorders. Maternal obesity predisposes to rupture through impaired wound healing driven by chronic inflammation and proinflammatory cytokines, alongside dysfunctional labor patterns and reduced contractility. Clinical monitoring employing continuous fetal heart rate tracing, high-resolution ultrasound, and strict labor management protocols effectively reduces asymptomatic rupture cases, though 88% of study participants presented in active labor with minimal warning signs such as cardiotocography abnormalities or fetal position changes during labor.

title: Development and validation of a postpartum cardiovascular disease risk prediction model in women incorporating reproductive and pregnancy-related predictors
summary: This study developed and validated an enhanced cardiovascular disease risk prediction model for postpartum women by incorporating reproductive and pregnancy-related factors into the QRISK®-3 algorithm. Multiple pregnancy complications—including gestational hypertension, preeclampsia, preterm birth, placental abruption, gestational diabetes mellitus, and small for gestational age—showed strong associations with increased postpartum cardiovascular risk. Additionally, reproductive factors such as polycystic ovary syndrome, endometriosis, menstrual irregularity, and early menarche were independently associated with cardiovascular disease risk. Although adding pregnancy and reproductive factors to traditional CVD risk factors (age, ethnicity, deprivation, blood pressure, cholesterol, smoking status, and body mass index) produced only marginal improvements in model discrimination, the updated model demonstrated better calibration in specific subgroups, particularly women with histories of preeclampsia, postnatal depression, and preterm birth. These findings underscore the postpartum period as a critical window for implementing preventive cardiovascular interventions in women with adverse pregnancy histories or reproductive conditions.

title: Endometriosis and associations with risks of adverse pregnancy and perinatal outcomes: a case-control study in Egypt
summary:
# Summary

This Egyptian case-control study demonstrates that endometriosis significantly increases risks for multiple adverse pregnancy and perinatal outcomes, with placenta previa, postpartum hemorrhage, preterm birth, and stillbirth being particularly prominent complications. The underlying mechanisms involve hormonal imbalances driven by elevated estrogen levels, along with pathological changes including progesterone-resistant endometrium, inflammatory mediators (cyclooxygenase-2, prostaglandin E2, interleukin-8), uterine hyperperistalsis, and pelvic adhesions that collectively impair normal placentation and embryo implantation. Notably, women with endometriosis who underwent surgical treatment prior to pregnancy showed markedly higher placenta previa incidence (36% versus 7% in controls), suggesting that surgical adhesions and disease recurrence compound pregnancy risks. In contrast, patients receiving only medical or hormonal therapy did not demonstrate significantly elevated adverse outcomes, indicating that treatment modality substantially influences pregnancy prognosis. The disease severity matters considerably, with deep infiltrating and stage IV endometriosis showing stronger associations with placental complications than milder forms.

title: The impact of assisted reproductive technologies versus natural conception on neonatal intensive care unit admission: A retrospective cohort analysis
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective cohort study demonstrates that neonates conceived through assisted reproductive technologies (ART) paradoxically experience significantly lower rates of neonatal intensive care unit (NICU) admission compared to naturally conceived neonates, despite being born at shorter gestational ages with lower birth weights. The protective effect of ART appears to be mediated partially (18.98%) by higher cesarean delivery rates, while enhanced prenatal monitoring, selective single embryo transfer, and stringent embryo selection through morphological and genetic screening enable earlier detection and management of complications. Additionally, ART-conceived embryos may be inherently healthier and better able to tolerate shorter gestations due to rigorous selection processes. The study suggests that comprehensive ART-related practices, combined with the demographic characteristics of ART users (higher socioeconomic status, greater health awareness, and better healthcare access) and the elimination of multiple pregnancy risks through elective single embryo transfer, collectively contribute to improved neonatal outcomes despite adverse obstetric parameters typically associated with poorer prognosis.

title: Correlation between multiple embryo transfers and the incidence of preterm birth and low birth weight: a network meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

This network meta-analysis demonstrates that multiple embryo transfers (ETs) during assisted reproductive technology are associated with significantly increased risks of preterm birth and low birth weight, with four-embryo transfers (4-ET) showing the highest adverse outcome rates. Clinical pregnancy rates paradoxically decline with increasing transfer number, with 2-ET achieving a 45.2% pregnancy rate compared to only 36.5% for 4-ET, suggesting diminishing returns from additional transfers. The mechanisms underlying these adverse pregnancy outcomes likely involve cumulative alterations in endometrial receptivity and placental function as transfer frequency increases. Based on cumulative ranking analysis, 2-ET emerged as the optimal strategy with an 82.4% SUCRA probability of being best, balancing acceptable pregnancy success with minimized risk of preterm birth and low birth weight. Since endometriosis accounts for 15% of IVF indications and approximately 28% of women require multiple transfer attempts, this finding highlights the importance of limiting unnecessary embryo transfers to improve long-term neonatal health outcomes.

title: Active chromatin marks and up-regulation of FOXC1 in uterine epithelial cells demarcate the onset of reproductive decline in aging females
summary: Advanced maternal age impairs reproductive outcomes through age-related changes in the uterine environment independent of oocyte quality, manifesting as increased miscarriage, stillbirth, preterm birth, and congenital abnormalities. Uterine aging is characterized by progressive accumulation of activating histone marks (H3K27ac and H3K4me3) in epithelial cells that converge on dramatic upregulation of the transcription factor FOXC1, which drives profound transcriptomic shifts and increased epithelial proliferation. FOXC1 hyperactivation causes glandular hyperplasia and dysregulation of critical factors like SOX9 that are essential for normal uterine gland maturity and function. Additionally, advanced maternal age reduces uterine responsiveness to progesterone, leading to deficits in endometrial stromal decidualization and impaired pregnancy establishment, a mechanism also observed in endometriosis-associated reproductive failure. The study identifies activating epigenetic marks and FOXC1 upregulation as molecular hallmarks demarcating the onset of age-induced reproductive decline, with disrupted gland-stromal crosstalk contributing to developmental abnormalities and adverse pregnancy outcomes. These findings reveal that uterine epithelial dysfunction and epigenetic remodeling during aging compromise the uterine environment's capacity to support successful pregnancy independent of embryonic factors.

title: Association Between Women’s Birth Weight and Reproductive Characteristics in Adulthood: The JPHC-NEXT Study
summary:
# Summary

This Japanese epidemiological study examined how birth weight relates to reproductive characteristics across the adult lifespan, finding that women born with low birth weight (<2,500 g) exhibit substantially altered reproductive timing and fertility outcomes. Women with very low birth weight (<1,500 g) experienced later menarche onset, earlier menopause, a shortened reproductive span of approximately 7-8 months, and higher rates of menstrual irregularity and nulliparity compared to normal birth weight controls. These reproductive disruptions likely stem from impaired in utero development affecting ovarian structure and function, including reduced ovarian and uterine size, altered gonadotropin secretion, and decreased primordial follicle numbers in girls born small for gestational age. Notably, the adverse associations between low birth weight and reproductive outcomes were more pronounced in women born before 1960, suggesting that early life nutritional and environmental conditions substantially influence lifetime reproductive health. The findings underscore how prenatal developmental constraints create long-lasting effects on reproductive function, with potential implications for understanding adverse pregnancy outcomes and fertility complications in women with low birth weight histories.

title: Comparative Assessment of Uterocervical Angle Using Transvaginal, Transabdominal, and Transperineal Ultrasonography Between 16 and 24 Weeks of Gestation
summary:
# Summary

Preterm birth is a significant clinical concern associated with severe neonatal complications including intraventricular hemorrhage, bronchopulmonary dysplasia, retinopathy of prematurity, and necrotizing enterocolitis, which can be reduced through cervical cerclage, progesterone supplementation, and tocolysis. The uterocervical angle (UCA), measured via transvaginal ultrasound (TVUS) between 16 and 24 weeks of gestation, has emerged as a novel predictor of spontaneous preterm birth, with wider angles indicating increased risk, and can be used alongside cervical length and immune-mediated factors to improve prediction accuracy. While transabdominal ultrasound (TAUS) showed moderate correlation and consistency with the gold-standard TVUS method and offers superior patient acceptability, transperineal ultrasound (TPUS) demonstrated substantial variability and poor agreement with TVUS due to technical limitations including tissue artifacts, insonation angles, and sensitivity to probe positioning. TAUS produced systematically lower UCA measurements than TVUS (median difference −8°), whereas TPUS showed minimal bias (median difference 3°) but poor reliability; consequently, TAUS may serve as a practical alternative for population-level research, while TPUS measurements lack sufficient precision for standalone clinical use in preterm birth prediction.

title: First-Trimester Uterine Artery Doppler Indices and Pregnancy Outcomes in Naturally Conceived and Frozen–Thawed Embryo Transfer Cycles
summary:
# Summary

This study examined first-trimester uterine artery Doppler indices as markers of vascular adaptation in frozen-thawed embryo transfer (FET) cycles compared to naturally conceived pregnancies. The findings demonstrate that oral dydrogesterone produces significantly lower uterine artery pulsatility index (PI) values compared to micronized progesterone or natural conception, suggesting improved uterine blood flow and placental adaptation. Lower PI values are associated with successful spiral artery remodeling and improved uteroplacental hemodynamics, outcomes critical for preventing adverse pregnancy complications including preeclampsia and fetal growth restriction. Dydrogesterone's favorable vascular effects appear mediated through modulation of endometrial immune signaling and cytokine expression, promoting an anti-inflammatory maternal response conducive to pregnancy maintenance. Notably, maternal body mass index emerged as an independent negative predictor of PI, independently influencing uterine vascular resistance, though this relationship presents a paradox as obesity is also independently associated with abnormal placentation. These results suggest that tailoring progesterone supplementation regimens in assisted reproduction, particularly toward dydrogesterone, may confer hemodynamic advantages during early pregnancy and support improved uteroplacental adaptation in FET cycles.

title: Doppler abnormality predisposes preterm infants with fetal growth restriction to postnatal intestinal disorder
journal: The Journal of Obstetrics and Gynaecology Research
summary:
# Summary

Placental insufficiency triggers a cascade of fetal complications that predispose preterm infants to postnatal intestinal disorders, with prolonged Doppler abnormalities emerging as a critical predictor. Chronic intrauterine stress from placental insufficiency causes fetal blood flow redistribution away from the gastrointestinal tract toward the brain (brain-sparing effect), detectable through ultrasound markers including umbilical artery absent or reversed end-diastolic flow. This hemodynamic compromise creates intestinal vulnerability through multiple mechanisms: impaired blood flow causes ischemia, intestinal immaturity remains uncorrected, and inflammatory cascades become dysregulated, collectively predisposing infants to necrotizing enterocolitis, focal intestinal perforation, and meconium-related ileus—all conditions associated with high mortality rates. The study demonstrates that fetal growth restriction occurred more frequently in infants who subsequently developed intestinal disorders (45.5% versus 23.7%), and importantly, the duration of Doppler abnormalities was prolonged in the intestinal disorder group, suggesting that sustained fetal compromise rather than isolated growth restriction drives postnatal intestinal pathology. Anti-inflammatory interventions such as probiotics and human milk oligosaccharides may help mitigate intestinal damage in this high-risk population.

title: The role of 25(OH)D3 levels in gestational diabetes and its outcomes
summary: Low serum 25(OH)D3 levels are significantly associated with increased incidence of gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) and represent an independent predictor of GDM risk in pregnant women. Vitamin D deficiency promotes GDM development through multiple mechanisms, including impaired insulin secretion and sensitivity, reduced insulin receptor expression, and dysregulation of glucose metabolism mediated by the vitamin D receptor expressed in pancreatic, hepatic, muscle, and adipose tissues. Beyond GDM, low 25(OH)D3 levels are strongly associated with increased rates of maternal complications including gestational hypertension, insulin resistance, and elevated fasting blood glucose, as well as adverse fetal and neonatal outcomes such as fetal distress, low birth weight, respiratory distress syndrome, jaundice, and higher cesarean section rates. Vitamin D also functions in immune modulation and inflammation regulation, which may contribute to protecting pancreatic beta cell function and reducing pregnancy complications. The evidence suggests that vitamin D supplementation may have clinical value in preventing and treating GDM, improving maternal metabolic control and reducing associated pregnancy adverse outcomes. These findings establish vitamin D deficiency as an important modifiable risk factor for GDM and related pregnancy complications.

title: Development and Validation of An Interpretable Machine Learning-Based Prediction Model of Postpartum Hemorrhage in Placenta Previa Following Cesarean Section: A Multicenter Study
summary: This multicenter study developed and validated a machine learning prediction model for postpartum hemorrhage (PPH) in patients with placenta previa undergoing cesarean delivery. Placenta previa and placental accreta spectrum disorders are major direct causes of PPH, with risk amplified by maternal factors including advanced age, multiparity, prior cesarean delivery, and uterine scarring. The Gradient Boosting Machine model emerged as the superior predictive tool, identifying five key variables that promote PPH: elevated d-dimer, ultrasound-confirmed placental accreta spectrum, prolonged prothrombin time, elevated neutrophils, and reduced platelet count, with d-dimer demonstrating the strongest predictive importance. Coagulation and inflammatory markers (d-dimer, prothrombin time, activated partial thromboplastin time, and neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio) were significantly elevated in PPH cases, while platelet counts were correspondingly lower. The model integrating these variables demonstrated excellent discriminative performance for identifying high-risk pregnancies, offering clinicians an interpretable tool to anticipate and prepare for PPH complications in the high-risk placenta previa population.

title: Diagnosis of postpartum depression and associated factors in South Africa: a cohort study of 47,697 women
summary: This large South African cohort study of 47,697 women identified multiple prenatal, obstetric, and psychosocial risk factors for postpartum depression (PPD). Prior depression history was the strongest predictor, with an adjusted relative risk of 3.47, while pregnancy and obstetric complications including preterm delivery, polycystic ovary syndrome, hyperemesis gravidarum, gestational hypertension, gestational diabetes, preeclampsia, perineal laceration, and both elective and emergency cesarean sections all significantly increased PPD risk. Endometriosis emerged as an additional gynecological risk factor for PPD, alongside socioeconomic and psychosocial determinants such as lower education, unwanted pregnancy, intimate partner violence, poor family support, low income, and unemployment. Notably, treatment of gestational hypertension reduced PPD risk to comparable levels with unaffected women, suggesting clinical intervention may mitigate depression risk. The consequences of PPD extended beyond maternal mental and physical health to impair infant development across motor, cognitive, emotional, and behavioral domains. These findings underscore the complex etiology of PPD and highlight opportunities for targeted screening and intervention in women with identified risk factors.

title: Lifestyle Modifications Prior to Pregnancy and Their Impact on Maternal and Perinatal Outcomes: A Review
summary:
# Summary

Preconception lifestyle modifications substantially improve maternal and perinatal outcomes through multiple biological pathways. Weight management, smoking and alcohol cessation, increased physical activity, and dietary improvements with adequate folic acid supplementation reduce risks of gestational diabetes mellitus, fetal growth restriction, neural tube defects, and preterm birth. Mechanistically, preconception weight loss reduces inflammatory profiles and improves metabolic function and fertility, while smoking and high body mass index impair oocyte quality in assisted reproductive settings. For women with specific conditions like type 1 diabetes and polycystic ovary syndrome, tight glycemic control and metformin therapy combined with lifestyle modifications optimize both maternal and fetal outcomes, with each 0.5% decline in hemoglobin A1c reducing congenital anomalies by six percent and severe maternal morbidity by ten percent. Population-level interventions including dietary counseling, structured physical activity programs, and public health campaigns demonstrate efficacy in reducing adverse outcomes, highlighting the critical importance of comprehensive preconception care in improving long-term health trajectories for both pregnant individuals and their offspring.

title: A prediction model of preterm birth in singleton pregnancy after assisted reproduction therapy
summary:
# Summary

Assisted reproductive technology (ART) is associated with increased preterm birth risk in singleton pregnancies, with IVF/ICSI conferring a 1.72-fold increased odds ratio. Multiple independent risk factors for preterm birth after ART were identified, including the number of embryos transferred, polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), intrauterine adhesion (IA), gestational hypertensive disease (GHD), and premature rupture of membranes (PROM), with PROM showing the strongest association (OR = 4.18). Pregnancies resulting in preterm birth demonstrated significantly higher prevalence of PCOS, IA, GHD, PROM, and placenta previa compared to full-term pregnancies, along with reduced cervical length after 16 weeks of gestation and lower basal estradiol levels. Preterm infants face substantial morbidity including developmental delays, cognitive impairments, respiratory complications, neurodevelopmental disorders, and cerebral palsy, alongside reduced birth weight and Apgar scores and increased rates of cesarean delivery and NICU admission. The study establishes cervical length monitoring as a potential predictive tool for preterm birth, particularly after the second trimester, supporting targeted clinical interventions in ART pregnancies.

title: Obstetric outcomes following artificial and natural cycle frozen embryo transfer in patients with and without endometriosis: a national cohort study
summary: Endometriosis is a common cause of infertility associated with multiple adverse obstetric complications, including placenta previa, postpartum hemorrhage, placental abruption, and low birth weight, through mechanisms involving pelvic adhesions, progesterone resistance, estrogen predominance, and inflammation-mediated damage from reactive oxygen species. This national cohort study compared artificial cycle (AC) frozen embryo transfer (FET) using estradiol with natural cycle (NC) FET, finding that AC-FET significantly increased risks of threatened abortion, hypertensive disorders of pregnancy, placenta previa, and cesarean delivery in endometriosis patients compared to NC-FET. Notably, natural cycle FET substantially reduced the risk of threatened abortion (21.0% versus 35.1%) and recurrent pregnancy loss (5.7% versus 9.4%) in endometriosis patients. In non-endometriosis patients, AC-FET was also associated with increased risks of miscarriage, hypertensive disorders, and postpartum hemorrhage, though with generally lower absolute risks than in endometriosis patients. These findings suggest that endometrial preparation method selection may influence pregnancy outcomes differentially based on endometriosis status, with natural cycle protocols appearing more favorable for endometriosis patients undergoing FET.

title: Combined vaginal progesterone and cervical Pessary effect on preterm birth in Singleton pregnancies with short cervix: a retrospective cohort study
summary: This retrospective cohort study demonstrates that combined vaginal progesterone and cervical pessary therapy significantly reduces preterm birth risk in singleton pregnancies with shortened cervical length compared to progesterone monotherapy. Short cervix is a well-established risk factor for spontaneous preterm birth, which progesterone mitigates through anti-inflammatory and uterine-quiescence effects while the pessary provides mechanical cervical support. The combination therapy reduced preterm birth before 34 weeks (3.1% vs. 11.2%) and before 37 weeks (22.4% vs. 37.3%), with substantially improved neonatal outcomes including fewer NICU admissions, lower resuscitation rates, higher birth weights, and shorter NICU stays. Maternal complications were also noticeably lower in the combination group with no increased risk of gestational diabetes, preeclampsia, or anxiety. These findings suggest that dual mechanistic intervention—combining progesterone's pharmacologic effects with pessary's mechanical support—provides superior protection against preterm delivery and its associated neonatal morbidities including respiratory distress syndrome, intraventricular hemorrhage, and neurodevelopmental complications.

title: Development and Content of Core Outcome Sets in Neonatology—A Scoping Review
summary:
# Summary

This scoping review examines core outcome sets (COS) in neonatology, which standardize outcome selection and reporting across clinical trials and observational studies to improve research synthesis. The five most frequently included outcomes in neonatal COS are neonatal mortality, preterm birth, birth weight, neonatal intensive care unit admission, and neurodevelopmental assessment, alongside condition-specific outcomes such as sepsis, necrotizing enterocolitis, retinopathy of prematurity, and bronchopulmonary dysplasia. Core outcome sets are typically developed through systematic literature searches combined with modified Delphi consensus methods involving neonatologists, parent representatives, researchers, policy makers, and regulators, with median round numbers of three. A significant challenge identified is outcome variability and inconsistent definitions across studies, exemplified by conditions like bronchopulmonary dysplasia and sepsis, though stakeholder groups including patients, parents, clinicians, nurses, and researchers consistently rank mortality and necrotizing enterocolitis as top priorities. The COMET initiative has provided a standardized methodological framework for COS development, though outcomes range from very specific conditions like vasa previa to broader neonatal intensive care quality measures, and parents often prioritize quality of life measures more highly than clinicians value health complications alone.

title: IVF and pregnancy outcomes: the triumphs, challenges, and unanswered questions
summary:
# Summary: IVF and Pregnancy Outcomes

IVF pregnancies carry substantially elevated risks for multiple adverse outcomes, including preterm birth, gestational complications, and congenital malformations compared to naturally conceived pregnancies. While IVF accounts for only 2% of live births in the USA, it contributes disproportionately to 5.1% of preterm births, a risk that persists even in singleton IVF pregnancies and is partly mediated by preeclampsia and underlying maternal conditions such as polycystic ovary syndrome and endometriosis. Multiple embryo transfer historically increased preeclampsia and multiple pregnancy rates, though the adoption of elective single embryo transfer and improved cryopreservation techniques has substantially reduced multiple pregnancies; however, frozen embryo transfers prepared with artificial cycles carry higher preeclampsia risk that can be mitigated by ovulatory endometrial preparation and aspirin prophylaxis. IVF pregnancies also show elevated risks for gestational diabetes mellitus (particularly with fresh embryo transfer and non-ICSI procedures), placental complications including previa and morbidly adherent placenta, and congenital malformations spanning cardiovascular, neurological, and musculoskeletal systems. These adverse outcomes are influenced by both the IVF procedure itself—including supraphysiologic hormone exposure affecting placental development—and underlying maternal characteristics such as advanced maternal age, obesity, and chronic hypertension, particularly in oocyte donation pregnancies.

title: Blastocyst quality and congenital malformation risk in singleton births after frozen embryo transfer
summary:
# Summary

This propensity score-matched cohort study of 1,743 frozen-thawed single blastocyst transfer cycles examined whether blastocyst morphological quality predicts adverse neonatal and obstetric outcomes. Contrary to concerns raised in prior literature, the researchers found no significant association between poor-quality blastocyst morphology and increased risk of congenital malformations, with comparable incidence rates between good- and poor-quality embryo groups. Similarly, blastocyst quality showed no meaningful differences in secondary outcomes including preterm birth, low birth weight, gestational age, birth weight, small or large for gestational age status, or maternal complications such as gestational diabetes and hypertension. The good-quality blastocyst group was characterized by younger patient age, higher anti-Müllerian hormone levels, lower follicle-stimulating hormone levels, and greater primary infertility prevalence. These findings suggest that morphological embryo assessment, while remaining standard clinical practice, has limited predictive value for congenital malformation risk and other adverse pregnancy outcomes in frozen embryo transfer cycles.

title: Pregnancy and neonatal outcomes in women with arcuate uterus: a population-based cohort study of over 3.8 million women
summary:
# Summary

This population-based cohort study of over 3.8 million women demonstrates that arcuate uterus, a congenital uterine anomaly, is significantly associated with multiple adverse pregnancy and neonatal outcomes. Women with arcuate uterus experienced substantially elevated risks of cesarean delivery (aOR 30.04), placental abruption (aOR 2.11), premature preterm rupture of membranes (aOR 2.46), preterm delivery (aOR 2.74), preeclampsia (aOR 2.08), and disseminated intravascular coagulopathy (aOR 5.58), along with higher rates of small-for-gestational-age neonates and postpartum hemorrhage. The underlying mechanisms appear to involve unilateral placental implantation with functional exclusion of one uterine artery, leading to placental malperfusion and resultant intrauterine growth restriction and hypertensive complications. Additionally, the study notes that congenital uterine anomalies more broadly are associated with endometriosis, abnormal uterine bleeding, and pelvic pain in non-pregnant women, while Müllerian anomalies frequently co-occur with renal and cardiac defects. These findings highlight arcuate uterus as an important risk factor for adverse maternal and fetal outcomes requiring enhanced obstetric monitoring and management.

title: Infant Viability in Severe Preeclampsia: Management Strategies and the Potential Role of Calprotectin—A Narrative Review
summary:
# Summary

Preeclampsia is a vascular pregnancy disorder characterized by high blood pressure and proteinuria that results from defective placentation and endothelial dysfunction, ultimately causing placental insufficiency. This condition leads to severe complications including intrauterine growth restriction and premature delivery, affecting approximately 5% of pregnancies worldwide. The pathophysiology involves dysregulated placental blood flow and ischemia/hypoxia, which triggers release of anti-angiogenic factors like soluble FMS-like tyrosine kinase 1 (sFlt1) while reducing angiogenic factors such as placental growth factor, resulting in oxidative stress and increased mitochondrial reactive oxygen species production. Preeclampsia is characterized by maternal immune activation, including elevated calprotectin and interleukin-8 released from activated neutrophils, alongside increased placental and trophoblast apoptosis. Elevated calprotectin levels represent a potential inflammatory biomarker for preeclampsia screening, reflecting the underlying neutrophil-mediated inflammatory processes that contribute to the disease. Additionally, dysregulation of multiple immune-related genes (including CD40L, TNFRSF8, IL1R2, and CTLA4) and microRNAs impairs the inflammatory response and angiogenic balance, further contributing to placental dysfunction and adverse neonatal outcomes.

title: Systemic inflammation indices measured shortly before delivery are not associated with preterm birth in pregnant women with endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis, a chronic inflammatory condition affecting approximately 10% of reproductive-age women, is associated with elevated preterm birth risk, with 20% of pregnant women with endometriosis experiencing preterm birth compared to 11.4% in controls. While endometriosis characteristically involves elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines (interleukin-6 and tumor necrosis factor-alpha) and alterations to the uterine microenvironment that affect cytokine expression, immune cell populations, and decidualization, systemic inflammation indices measured shortly before delivery—including the systemic immune-inflammation index, neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio, and other peripheral blood markers—did not significantly differ between pregnant women with endometriosis who did or did not experience preterm birth. These findings suggest that while endometriosis increases preterm birth risk through local uterine immune dysfunction rather than systemic inflammation detectable in peripheral blood near delivery, local immune environmental changes within the uterus may be the primary mechanism driving adverse pregnancy outcomes in this population. The lack of association between measured systemic inflammatory indices and preterm birth indicates that the inflammatory pathophysiology of endometriosis-related preterm birth operates predominantly at the local tissue level rather than as a generalized systemic inflammatory process.

title: Reproductive outcomes after fertility-sparing interventions for symptomatic adenomyosis: a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary: Adenomyosis is strongly associated with reduced fertility, characterized by significantly lower implantation rates, clinical pregnancy rates, and live birth rates in affected women, while increasing spontaneous miscarriage risk and complications including preterm birth, pre-eclampsia, and postpartum hemorrhage. The pathophysiology involves ectopic endometrium within the myometrium that disrupts cellular, hormonal, and immunological environments, impairing critical reproductive processes including decidualization and placentation. This systematic review and meta-analysis demonstrates that fertility-sparing surgical interventions substantially improve reproductive outcomes, with adenomyomectomy achieving approximately 50% pregnancy rates and 40% delivery rates, though accompanied by high cesarean section rates (99.6%) and modest miscarriage rates (16.3%). Adenomyomectomy performed via laparoscopy or laparotomy significantly outperforms uterine artery embolization (UAE), which is associated with low pregnancy rates, elevated miscarriage, and postpartum hemorrhage risks.
