Image-guided thermal ablation techniques show comparable pregnancy rates (52%) to adenomyomectomy with lower adverse pregnancy outcomes (1.0% versus 21.4%), suggesting thermal approaches may offer enhanced safety profiles. These findings support fertility-sparing interventions as effective treatments for symptomatic adenomyosis in women desiring pregnancy, with surgical approaches and ablation techniques demonstrating promising reproductive restoration potential.

title: Clinical Benefits and Safety of Multiple Micronutrient Supplementation During Preconception, Pregnancy, and Lactation: A Review
summary:
# Summary

Multiple micronutrient supplementation (MMS) significantly improves overall micronutrient status and creates a healthier reproductive environment during the preconception, pregnancy, and lactation periods. Evidence from 30 trials demonstrates that MMS substantially reduces major adverse pregnancy outcomes, including neural tube defects, congenital abnormalities, miscarriage, preeclampsia, anemia, preterm birth, and placental insufficiency, with formulations containing 800 μg folic acid proving more effective than lower doses. The protective mechanisms involve key micronutrients—folate, vitamins B2, B6, B12, vitamin D, iron, zinc, and copper—which are essential for neural tube closure, cell division, and homocysteine metabolism, while also reducing oxidative stress and supporting brain development and postpartum mental health. By lowering homocysteine levels and improving docosahexaenoic acid status, MMS addresses critical risk factors linked to reproductive complications and supports the nutrient quality of breastmilk, though rare adverse events such as otitis media and atopic dermatitis were noted in one pediatric cohort.

title: Hypertensive disorders of pregnancy and gestational diabetes mellitus affect fetal growth and perinatal outcomes in women undergoing in vitro fertilization
summary:
# Summary

This study examines how hypertensive disorders of pregnancy (HDP) and gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) affect fetal growth and delivery outcomes in women undergoing in vitro fertilization. HDP causes systemic arteriolar spasms and compromised placental function, leading to small-for-gestational-age infants, preterm birth, and fetal growth restrictions, while GDM promotes excessive fetal glucose transfer that stimulates fetal insulin production, resulting in large-for-gestational-age infants and macrosomia. Women with HDP had significantly increased risks of preterm delivery and cesarean section compared to unaffected IVF pregnancies, whereas GDM pregnancies paradoxically showed higher risks of both small-for-gestational-age and large-for-gestational-age infants. The concurrent occurrence of HDP and GDM substantially elevated risks further, with dramatically increased odds ratios for small-for-gestational-age (6.37) and low birthweight (7.77). These findings indicate that both conditions independently and synergistically compromise perinatal outcomes in IVF populations, with HDP associated with an insulin resistance syndrome metabolic profile, suggesting shared pathophysiological mechanisms warrant targeted clinical interventions.

title: Causal Effects of Endometriosis Stages on Adverse Pregnancy and Perinatal Outcomes: A Mendelian Randomization Study
summary: This Mendelian randomization study demonstrates that endometriosis exerts stage-dependent causal effects on adverse pregnancy outcomes, with advanced disease (stage III-IV) showing the strongest associations. Advanced endometriosis significantly increases the risk of intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy, while early-stage disease (stage I-II) shows no significant causal relationships with major obstetric complications. Notably, the study found limited causal evidence for endometriosis causing other previously reported pregnancy complications such as preterm birth, gestational diabetes, pre-eclampsia, and postpartum hemorrhage, suggesting these associations may be confounded or mediated through other pathways. The analysis carefully excluded genetic variants associated with confounding factors including weight, BMI, smoking initiation, sex hormone levels, and allergic diseases to isolate the true causal effects of endometriosis. These findings highlight the importance of endometriosis staging in assessing pregnancy risk and suggest that only advanced-stage disease carries demonstrable causal effects on specific pregnancy complications, particularly hepatic cholestasis. The results underscore the multifactorial nature of adverse pregnancy outcomes and the need for stage-specific clinical management strategies in endometriosis patients.

title: Obstetric and perinatal outcomes in twin pregnancies conceived via assisted reproductive technology vs. spontaneous conception: a population-based cohort study
summary:
# Summary

This population-based cohort study demonstrates that twin pregnancies conceived via assisted reproductive technology (ART) experience significantly elevated risks across multiple adverse maternal and perinatal outcomes compared to spontaneously conceived twins. ART-conceived twin pregnancies showed increased associations with preeclampsia (aOR 1.31), gestational diabetes mellitus (aOR 1.32), preterm labor (aOR 1.20), intrauterant growth restriction (aOR 1.18), emergency cesarean delivery (aOR 1.21), postpartum hemorrhage (aOR 1.19), and threatened abortion (aOR 1.75), with particularly pronounced risks in women aged 35 years or older. The underlying mechanisms include excess placental mass, suboptimal placental implantation sites leading to placenta previa and velamentous cord insertion, placental crowding limiting uteroplacental capacity, and potential interference with implantation from embryo manipulation during ART procedures. Additionally, ART-conceived twins exhibited higher rates of neonatal intensive care unit admissions and perinatal mortality, with both monochorionic diamniotic and dichorionic diamniotic ART twin pregnancies showing reduced twin survival and lower birth weights. These findings highlight that while ART populations have distinct risk profiles due to advanced maternal age and underlying infertility-related comorbidities including endometriosis, the procedure itself and associated placental abnormalities substantially compound adverse outcomes in twin gestations beyond inherent twin pregnancy risks.

title: Non-genetic risk factors of miscarriage: a comprehensive umbrella review of systematic review and meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

This comprehensive umbrella review identifies multiple non-genetic risk factors associated with miscarriage across behavioral, medical, infectious, and occupational domains. Modifiable lifestyle factors including smoking, alcohol consumption, obesity, and vitamin D deficiency show strong associations with miscarriage risk, as do endocrine disorders such as hypothyroidism and diabetes, which can be partially mitigated through levothyroxine treatment. Structural and infectious contributors include uterine abnormalities, congenital uterine defects, microbial infections (particularly parvovirus B19 and Chlamydia trachomatis), and inflammatory bowel diseases, all demonstrating elevated miscarriage odds. The review also evaluates specific medications, revealing that certain antibiotics (tetracycline, quinolones, macrolides, clarithromycin, and metronidazole), lithium, and statins are associated with increased miscarriage risk, while penicillin, cephalosporins, and amoxicillin appear safer alternatives. Additionally, occupational and physical stressors such as lifting weights ≥11 kg and night work contribute modestly to miscarriage risk, highlighting the multifactorial nature of pregnancy loss.

title: Are Dichorionic Twin Pregnancies Resulting from In Vitro Fertilization Different from Spontaneous Dichorionic Twin Pregnancies? A Retrospective Cohort Study
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective cohort study compared dichorionic/diamniotic twin pregnancies resulting from in vitro fertilization (IVF) with those from spontaneous conception, revealing substantially elevated perinatal risks in the IVF group. IVF pregnancies demonstrated significantly higher rates of preterm birth (59.8% versus 30.4%), fetal growth restriction (31.3% versus 19.5%), lower birth weights (2200g versus 2565g), and greater need for emergency cesarean sections (81.8% versus 55.8%) and tocolytic intervention (56.6% versus 29.7%). While maternal factors such as advanced age and higher primiparity in the IVF cohort contribute to these complications, the disparity persists, suggesting IVF-specific placental and vascular dysfunction as a mechanism. However, despite higher antepartum morbidity, neonatal outcomes—including Apgar scores, neonatal mortality, congenital anomalies, and twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome incidence—were comparable between groups, indicating that accelerated delivery prevents severe neonatal compromise in IVF twin pregnancies.

title: Awareness of Chorioamnionitis and Its Complications: A Cross-Sectional Study Among Women in Makkah City
summary:
# Summary

Chorioamnionitis, inflammation of the placental membranes caused primarily by ascending bacterial infections, represents a significant pregnancy complication with multiple well-established risk factors including prolonged membrane rupture, extended labor, nulliparity, internal labor monitoring, and infections such as group B streptococcus, bacterial vaginosis, and Ureaplasma. The condition is strongly associated with severe maternal and fetal complications, including respiratory distress syndrome, perinatal death, cesarean delivery, low birth weight, and neonatal infections such as meningitis and cerebral palsy. Women with chorioamnionitis experience substantially worse pregnancy outcomes, including fewer deliveries at 24 weeks gestation and increased early miscarriage rates. A cross-sectional survey of women in Makkah City revealed that awareness of chorioamnionitis varies significantly by demographic factors, with higher education, employment status, personal pregnancy history, and previous experience with pregnancy complications substantially increasing awareness levels. Healthcare workers and social media emerged as the primary information sources, though awareness rates among housewives and those with lower education remained suboptimal, highlighting the need for targeted educational interventions to improve public health literacy regarding this serious pregnancy complication.

title: Pregnancy in women with advanced endometriosis and adenomyosis: possible complications and the role of surgery
summary: Advanced endometriosis and adenomyosis are associated with multiple serious pregnancy complications, including miscarriage, preterm birth, fetal growth restriction, pre-eclampsia, placenta previa, and placental abruption. Deep infiltrating endometriosis carries particularly elevated risks for miscarriage, cesarean delivery, and placental complications, whereas ovarian endometriomas show minimal association with adverse outcomes. The underlying mechanisms involve chronic inflammatory states, impaired angiogenic signaling, and defective spiral artery remodeling that compromise placental development and function. Notably, surgical treatment of endometriosis does not consistently reduce pregnancy complications and may paradoxically increase risks for certain outcomes such as preterm birth and placenta previa, particularly when deep posterior endometriosis or anterior infiltrating disease requires excision. Assisted reproductive technology and high body mass index emerge as independent risk factors compounding endometriosis-related pregnancy risks. These findings suggest that while surgery may be necessary for symptom management or fertility preservation, pregnancy management in women with advanced endometriosis requires specialized surveillance and counseling regarding expected complications.

title: Evaluation of the Combined Use of Elastography and Cervicometry With the E-Cervix Index to Predict Second Trimester Preterm Birth Risk
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluates the E-Cervix index, a combined elastography and cervicometry approach, for predicting second trimester preterm birth risk. Isthmic-cervical insufficiency (ICI) is a significant precursor to preterm birth, characterized by shortened cervical length and increased tissue elasticity, with women with ICI showing significantly higher preterm delivery rates (16% vs. 8%). The E-Cervix index quantifies cervical stiffness through integrated ultrasound assessment and demonstrates improved predictive accuracy for identifying high-risk pregnancies, with high strain ratios and low elasticity scores associated with elevated preterm birth risk. Early detection via combined elastography and cervicometry enables timely clinical interventions—including progesterone therapy, cerclage, and pessary placement—that reduce preterm deliveries and associated neonatal complications including morbidity, mortality, and neurodevelopmental disabilities. The study underscores cervical measurement's critical value in adverse pregnancy outcome prediction, though challenges remain including operator training requirements and the need for multi-center validation studies to standardize elastography protocols.

title: Development and validation of a novel prediction model for hypertensive disorders of pregnancy based on maternal cardiovascular function and placental blood flow metrics at 22 to 24 gestational weeks using machine learning
summary:
# Summary

This study developed and validated a machine learning prediction model for hypertensive disorders of pregnancy (HDP) using maternal cardiovascular function and placental blood flow measurements obtained at 22-24 weeks of gestation. The researchers identified that impaired maternal cardiovascular hemodynamics, endothelial dysfunction, and abnormal placental function are central pathophysiological mechanisms underlying HDP, which are major causes of adverse maternal and perinatal outcomes. An optimized XGBoost model incorporating seven physiological features demonstrated that systolic blood pressure and systemic vascular resistance were the strongest predictive factors, with elevated values associating with increased HDP risk, while higher cardiac output and uterine artery blood flow corresponded to reduced risk. Multiple maternal risk factors including chronic hypertension, chronic kidney disease, autoimmune conditions, obesity, nulliparity, and family history of preeclampsia were strongly associated with HDP development. The findings underscore that cardiovascular maladaptation to pregnancy combined with placental dysfunction drives HDP pathogenesis, and suggest that mid-gestation hemodynamic assessment could improve clinical prediction and enable earlier preventive interventions such as aspirin prophylaxis.

title: Impact of Enoxaparin on Maternal and Fetal Outcomes in In Vitro Fertilization (IVF) Pregnancies With Thyroid Autoimmunity: A Real-World, Single-Center, Retrospective, Observational Study
summary:
# Summary

Thyroid autoimmunity in women undergoing IVF creates a pro-inflammatory state through elevated anti-thyroid antibodies that activate complement and thrombotic cascades, leading to adverse pregnancy outcomes including miscarriage, preterm birth, intrauterine growth restriction, and hypertensive disorders. The pathophysiology involves immune dysregulation characterized by elevated Th1/Th17 cells, natural killer cells, and pro-inflammatory cytokines (interferon-gamma), alongside reduced regulatory T cells and anti-inflammatory cytokines (interleukin-4 and -10), which disrupts placentation and trophoblast invasion. Notably, levothyroxine does not improve pregnancy outcomes in euthyroid women with anti-thyroid antibodies, but enoxaparin (a low-molecular-weight heparin) demonstrated significant clinical benefit by reducing miscarriage, preterm birth, and intrauterine growth restriction rates through its dual anticoagulant and immunomodulatory properties, as well as promotion of placental growth factor production. In this single-center cohort, enoxaparin treatment achieved a 97.5% live birth rate and was well-tolerated without serious adverse effects, suggesting it represents an effective therapeutic intervention for anti-thyroid antibody-positive IVF pregnancies beyond conventional thyroid hormone replacement.

title: The impact of serum estradiol and progesterone levels during implantation on obstetrical complications and perinatal outcomes in frozen embryo transfer
journal: Journal of the Chinese Medical Association : JCMA
summary:
# Summary

This study examined how serum estradiol and progesterone levels during embryo implantation affect obstetrical outcomes in frozen embryo transfer (FET) cycles. Frozen embryo transfer overall is associated with increased risks of pregnancy-induced hypertension, large for gestational age, postpartum hemorrhage, and preterm delivery. Natural cycle FET (NC-FET), which produces significantly higher endogenous estradiol and progesterone levels, substantially reduces risks of hypertensive disorders, preeclampsia, macrosomia, and cesarean section compared to hormone therapy cycle FET (HT-FET). In contrast, HT-FET is linked to elevated rates of hypertensive disorders, placenta accreta, postpartum hemorrhage, and macrosomia. The study found that abnormal hormone levels may impair placentation through multiple mechanisms: excessive estradiol exposure causes epigenetic changes in developing embryos, while low progesterone induces excessive trophoblast invasion leading to placenta accreta. However, the authors concluded that estradiol and progesterone levels around implantation do not appear to be the primary drivers of the observed differences in obstetric and perinatal complications between FET protocols.

title: The anterior cervical angle: A novel predictor of vaginal birth
journal: International Journal of Gynaecology and Obstetrics
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies the anterior cervical angle (ACA), measured via transvaginal ultrasound, as a novel predictor of vaginal delivery success and labor induction failure. Patients achieving vaginal delivery had significantly higher mean ACA (108.02°) compared to those undergoing cesarean section (86.11°), with an optimal ACA threshold of 96° demonstrating 70% sensitivity for predicting progression failure. The ACA appears to operate as a mechanical determinant of cervical biomechanics and fetal descent, capturing positional dynamics not fully assessed by traditional measures like Bishop score or cervical length. Multiple maternal and fetal factors contribute to cesarean delivery risk, including primiparity, increased maternal weight and BMI, and male fetal gender—the latter associated with larger head circumference and reduced placental oxygenation capacity. The research suggests that cervical maturity, maternal characteristics, and fetal position collectively influence labor induction success, with the ACA potentially serving as a more sensitive predictor of adverse labor outcomes including preterm birth and failed progression than conventional cervical assessments alone.

title: Triage and care for women with symptoms or diagnosis of pregnancy loss between 14 + 0 and 21 + 6 weeks' gestation
journal: International Journal of Gynaecology and Obstetrics
summary: Mid-trimester pregnancy loss (MTL), defined as pregnancy loss between 14 and 21 weeks' gestation, is associated with multiple demographic, behavioral, anatomical, and medical risk factors. Demographic risk factors include advanced maternal age (with risk increasing substantially after age 35, reaching an odds ratio of 8.9 for women ≥44 years), Black ethnicity, and a previous history of MTL. Behavioral and metabolic risk factors include active smoking (particularly 11-20 cigarettes daily), heavy alcohol consumption (≥3 drinks daily), and obesity (BMI ≥30 kg/m²), while anatomical factors encompassing cervical insufficiency, short cervix, congenital uterine anomalies, and myomas are strongly associated with MTL risk. Medical factors including infections, thrombophilias, thyroid disease, and polycystic ovary syndrome also contribute to MTL, along with fetal chromosomal abnormalities and placental dysfunction. Management of threatened MTL is tailored to the underlying etiology and may include progesterone supplementation, cervical cerclage for cervical insufficiency, and treatment of identified infections with antibiotics, with recurrence risk varying by the cause of the initial loss.

title: Risk of Placenta Accreta Spectrum With Early Pregnancy Subchorionic Hematoma Location and Size in Hormone Replacement Cycles
summary:
# Summary

Hormone replacement cycles significantly increase the risk of placenta accreta spectrum (PAS), primarily through an elevated incidence of subchorionic hematoma (SCH), particularly in the cervical region. Assisted reproductive technology, especially frozen-thawed embryo transfer, substantially increases SCH formation, which itself is associated with multiple adverse pregnancy outcomes including miscarriage, placental abruption, and preterm premature rupture of membranes. Notably, the anatomical location rather than size of SCH determines PAS risk, with cervical-side hematomas conferring significantly elevated risk while fundal-side and mid-cavity lesions showed no significant association. This location-specific effect is mechanistically explained by the cervical region's complex vascular network and high collagen content, which render it particularly vulnerable to vascular injury; in hormone replacement cycles, the absence of a corpus luteum and early exogenous estrogen-induced vascular dilation (versus the gradual physiological remodeling in natural cycles) compromise vascular integrity and predispose to hematoma formation. These findings suggest that endometrial vascular pathophysiology in assisted reproduction is a critical determinant of both early pregnancy complications and later PAS development.

title: A Machine Learning Model Based on First-Trimester Lipidomic Signatures for Predicting Metabolic Pregnancy Complications
summary:
# Summary

This study developed machine learning models leveraging first-trimester lipidomic signatures to predict metabolic pregnancy complications including gestational diabetes mellitus and fetal macrosomia. For gestational diabetes mellitus, elevated triacylglycerols (particularly TG 55:7, 55:8, 58:10, 51:5, and 49:3) and reduced phosphatidylcholine species served as key biomarkers, enabling an XGBoost model to achieve 87% sensitivity and 89% specificity. Fetal macrosomia was characterized by elevated phosphatidylglycerol, 4-hydroxybutyric acid, and pantothenol, with a Random Forest model achieving 87% sensitivity and 93% specificity. Beyond lipid alterations, risk factors including obesity, prior gestational diabetes history, endometriosis, and assisted reproduction were significantly associated with these complications, which carry serious long-term maternal consequences such as progression to type 2 diabetes, metabolic syndrome, and cardiovascular disease. These findings demonstrate that lipidomic profiling combined with clinical parameters can effectively identify pregnancies at risk for metabolic complications early in gestation.

title: Pregnancy and Neonatal Outcomes for Women Without Male Partners Undergoing Fertility Care via Intrauterine Insemination: A Retrospective Cohort Study
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective cohort study examined pregnancy and neonatal outcomes in women without male partners undergoing intrauterine insemination with donor sperm compared to women with male partners. Women without male partners were significantly older (median age 42 versus 38 years) and predominantly lacked common female infertility diagnoses, though they used cryopreserved donor sperm with lower motility. Despite these differences, clinical pregnancy rates, live birth rates, and miscarriage rates were comparable between groups, with similar neonatal outcomes including birthweight, Apgar scores, and NICU admission rates. However, women without male partners showed a trend toward increased hypertensive disorders of pregnancy, including preeclampsia with severe features and HELLP syndrome, potentially explained by impaired maternal immune adaptation from lack of prior exposure to paternal antigens. The authors attribute the overall favorable outcomes to the predominantly young ovarian age and absence of significant female infertility diagnoses in this population, despite the barriers of advanced maternal age and delays in sperm access.

title: Evaluation of Arcuate Uterus in Infertility Using 3D Transvaginal Ultrasound: A Retrospective Cohort Study
summary: This retrospective cohort study evaluated the clinical significance of arcuate uterus, a subtle uterine morphological abnormality resulting from incomplete Müllerian duct fusion or resorption, using 3D transvaginal ultrasound for precise diagnosis. Arcuate uterus was identified in 19% of infertile women and showed particularly strong association with unexplained infertility, occurring in approximately 32% of women in this subgroup. Women with arcuate uterus demonstrated higher prevalence of polycystic ovary syndrome, elevated body mass index, and endometrial polyps compared to controls, though these women had lower rates of identifiable female factor infertility. Notably, while arcuate uterus is theoretically associated with adverse pregnancy outcomes including implantation failure, recurrent miscarriage, preterm birth, and fetal growth restriction, actual live birth rates did not differ significantly between women with arcuate versus normal uteri. These findings suggest that although arcuate uterus represents a structural variation commonly encountered in infertile populations, its clinical impact on fertility outcomes may be less definitive than previously hypothesized, with potential underlying mechanisms involving altered endometrial morphology or vascular distribution.

title: Comparative Analysis Perinatal Complications Among Women with ART-Induced Pregnancy and Natural Conception
summary:
# Summary

This comparative analysis reveals that in vitro fertilization-embryo transfer (IVF-ET) pregnancies are associated with significantly elevated rates of multiple perinatal complications compared to naturally conceived pregnancies, including multiple gestations (24.3% vs 3.2%), anemia, postpartum hemorrhage, gestational hypertension, gestational diabetes mellitus, and intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy. The underlying mechanisms involve ovarian hyperstimulation leading to supraphysiological estrogen levels, which impairs endothelial function and promotes systemic inflammation, while in vitro embryo culture may induce epigenetic alterations affecting placental development. The cesarean delivery rate was markedly higher in the ART group (74.2%), though perineal laceration rates were paradoxically lower, and maternal age emerged as a critical risk factor, with women over 35 years showing significantly elevated risks of gestational diabetes and hypertensive disorders. Notably, ART patients often have underlying maternal pathologies such as endometriosis or tubal factor infertility associated with chronic inflammatory states, which may compound pregnancy complications. The study emphasizes that multiple pregnancies play a crucial role in precipitating these adverse outcomes and suggests that single embryo transfer strategies and enhanced monitoring protocols could mitigate these elevated perinatal risks.

title: Amniotic sheets: Imaging features, subtypes, and obstetric outcomes
summary:
# Summary

Amniotic sheets are intrauterine membranes that develop from uterine synechiae following endometrial trauma, such as curettage, myomectomy, or endometrial infection, with 32-78% of affected patients having prior uterine instrumentation. While amniotic sheets themselves are not associated with fetal malformations or entrapment, they are significantly linked to adverse obstetric outcomes including fetal malpresentation, cesarean delivery, preterm birth, low birth weight, and neonatal intensive care admission. Importantly, amniotic sheets with discontinuities or marked compartmentalization can create pathologic conditions where umbilical cord herniation through sheet defects may cause fetal asphyxiation, growth restriction, and intrauterine demise, with five reported cases of fetal death and evidence of asphyxia on postmortem examination. The mechanism of harm appears to involve mechanical obstruction of normal fetal movement and potential cord compromise, distinguishing compartmentalizing sheets from other intrauterine membranes like circumvallate placentae and uterine septa. Clinical recognition of amniotic sheet subtypes on imaging is critical for identifying pregnancies at risk for serious complications and guiding appropriate obstetric management.

title: First trimester antidepressant use and miscarriage: a population-based cohort study using Clinical Practice Research Datalink GOLD
journal: The British Journal of General Practice
summary: This population-based cohort study examined the relationship between first trimester antidepressant use and pregnancy outcomes in over one million pregnancies. While antidepressant prescription during early pregnancy was only modestly associated with miscarriage after adjustment for confounders, the study found a plausible biological mechanism: antidepressants inhibit serotonin transporters located on platelets, which may increase bleeding events and subsequently miscarriage risk. Notably, the association varied by dosage, with low and medium doses showing increased miscarriage risk while high doses showed an attenuated effect. The analysis revealed that incident (newly initiated) antidepressant use carried higher miscarriage risk than prevalent use, suggesting confounding by indication may be important. However, the study emphasizes a clinical dilemma: untreated depression and anxiety during pregnancy are themselves associated with adverse outcomes including miscarriage, preterm birth, and low birth weight. These findings underscore the complexity of antidepressant management in pregnancy and the need to balance risks of both treatment and untreated psychiatric illness.

title: Incidence of Retained Products of Conception Following Hormone Replacement Versus Natural Cycles in Frozen Embryo Transfer
summary:
# Summary

This study examined retained products of conception (RPOC) incidence following frozen embryo transfer in hormone replacement cycles (HRC) versus natural cycles (NC), finding that HRC was associated with significantly higher RPOC rates (5.4% versus 1.4%). The elevated risk in HRC likely stems from the absence of a functional corpus luteum, which normally produces progesterone, estrogen, and vasoactive peptides critical for proper endometrial receptivity, decidualization, and vascular remodeling. Exogenous hormone programming in HRC may impair endometrial gene expression and spiral artery remodeling, while prolonged hormone support could delay endometrial involution and dampen myometrial contractility, collectively impairing placental separation. Prior RPOC history and multiparity were the strongest independent predictors, while cesarean delivery, hypervascular retained tissue, elevated hCG levels, and early gestational age at pregnancy loss further increased risk. Diagnosis relies on transvaginal ultrasound with color Doppler assessment, complemented by serum hCG measurement and histopathological examination when available, with hysteroscopic removal emerging as the preferred treatment for symptomatic cases.

title: Immunohistochemical Characterization of the Immune Response in Chronic Endometritis Caused by Chlamydia trachomatis
summary: Chlamydia trachomatis infection is a leading preventable cause of chronic endometritis and is strongly associated with adverse pregnancy outcomes including miscarriage, preterm birth, low birth weight, and neonatal death, with infection prevalence in pregnant women ranging from 2% to 35%. Asymptomatic chlamydial infection can trigger recurrent first-trimester pregnancy loss through dysregulation of the immune response in the endometrium. Immunohistochemical analysis of endometrial tissue from women with C. trachomatis-induced chronic endometritis reveals a heightened inflammatory response characterized by infiltration of multiple immune cell populations including plasma cells, CD4+ and CD8+ T lymphocytes, B lymphocytes, natural killer cells, and macrophages. The infected endometrial tissue exhibits pathological changes including edema and hemorrhage, indicating significant tissue damage from the inflammatory response. These findings underscore the critical role of bacterial infection-induced endometrial inflammation in compromising pregnancy viability and highlight the importance of detecting and treating asymptomatic chlamydial infection in women of reproductive age to prevent adverse pregnancy outcomes.

title: Optimizing Maternal Microbiome: Role in Improved Conception and Pregnancy Outcome
summary:
# Summary

Maternal microbiome composition profoundly influences conception and pregnancy outcomes, with Lactobacillus crispatus dominance enhancing fertility while reducing miscarriage, preterm birth, and congenital infections. Dysbiosis—associated with obesity, antibiotic overuse, and poor nutrition—increases susceptibility to sexually transmitted infections and adverse pregnancy complications, including those caused by pathogens like chlamydia and gonorrhea that trigger endometritis and early pregnancy failure. Preconception optimization through screening, targeted antibiotic treatment of STIs, probiotic supplementation, and dietary interventions including Mediterranean diets significantly improves pregnancy outcomes and reduces infection-related pathology. Metabolic conditions including obesity, polycystic ovaries, and prediabetes further compromise fertility by altering vaginal and gut microbiota, though lifestyle modifications such as diet improvement, exercise, and BMI reduction can restore microbiome health and conception rates. Critical clinical considerations include managing antibiotic resistance through judicious use while preserving beneficial bacteria, and recognizing that medications like steroids and immunosuppressants similarly disrupt microbiota and immune defenses, necessitating preventive vaccination strategies to reduce harmful medication exposure during pregnancy.

title: Impact of assisted reproductive therapy on kidney and pregnancy outcomes in women with early-stage chronic kidney disease: a longitudinal analysis
summary: This longitudinal analysis examined how assisted reproductive therapy (ART) affects kidney and pregnancy outcomes in women with early-stage chronic kidney disease (CKD). The study found that CKD causes infertility primarily through disruption of the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis, where impaired gonadotropin-releasing hormone secretion prevents normal luteinizing hormone and follicle-stimulating hormone cycling, leading to anovulation and reduced estrogen production. While CKD is strongly associated with adverse pregnancy outcomes including preterm delivery, pre-eclampsia, and fetal growth restriction, particularly in advanced stages, the research revealed that ART appears safe for women with early-stage CKD and low risk of disease progression. Notably, the study found no increased risk of ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome or kidney function deterioration following ART in this population, though pregnancy itself imposes physiological stress through glomerular hyperfiltration that increases kidney burden. Common kidney manifestations in these women included glomerular hematuria and proteinuria, with underlying pathologies including IgA nephropathy and C3 glomerulonephritis. The authors conclude that ART can effectively treat infertility in selected women with early-stage CKD while maintaining stable renal function and acceptable maternal-neonatal outcomes.

title: The Creation of a Weight of Evidence Scoring Database for Risk Factors for Adverse Impacts to Birth Outcomes Using Expert Elicitation
summary: This study developed a weight of evidence scoring database identifying major risk factors for adverse birth outcomes through expert elicitation methodology. Maternal age emerged as the most consistently strong risk factor across multiple adverse outcomes, including fertility issues, miscarriage, neonatal death, preeclampsia, gestational diabetes, preterm birth, and congenital anomalies. Multiple pregnancy, severe obesity, and maternal body composition metrics (weight, height, BMI) demonstrated strong associations with numerous complications including preterm birth, abnormal fetal weight, preeclampsia, and growth restriction. Reproductive factors such as ovulatory dysfunction, oocyte quality, and previous pregnancy history showed substantial evidence for fertility problems and recurrent adverse outcomes, while infectious exposures including viral and sexually transmitted infections presented strong risks for congenital anomalies and pregnancy loss. This comprehensive evidence synthesis provides a quantitative framework for clinicians and researchers to assess cumulative risk profiles and prioritize interventions for pregnant individuals at elevated risk of adverse outcomes.

title: Association between the proportion of subchorionic hematoma within the gestational sac and miscarriage rate in IVF/ICSI patients
summary:
# Summary

This study examined subchorionic hematoma (SCH) as a risk factor for adverse pregnancy outcomes in IVF/ICSI patients. Patients with SCH demonstrated significantly higher miscarriage rates (13.5% versus 8.3%) and reduced live birth rates (86.5% versus 91.7%) compared to those without SCH. Notably, the proportion of SCH within the gestational sac emerged as a dose-dependent risk factor, with miscarriage rates progressively increasing as the hematoma proportion increased. Additional clinical observations revealed that miscarriage outcomes in SCH patients were associated with earlier hematoma detection, smaller gestational sac area, and transfer of more embryos. Beyond early pregnancy loss, SCH was correlated with late pregnancy complications including preterm birth, preeclampsia, postpartum hemorrhage, placental abruption, and lower birth weights. The findings suggest that SCH size and proportion warrant careful monitoring in early pregnancy, as they may serve as independent predictors of pregnancy viability in assisted reproduction settings.

title: Nonlinear association between pre-pregnancy body mass index and preterm birth in singleton pregnancies conceived with assisted reproductive technology
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals a nonlinear relationship between pre-pregnancy body mass index (BMI) and preterm birth risk in assisted reproductive technology (ART) pregnancies, with optimal BMI ranging from 21–23 kg/m². Both underweight (BMI <18.5 kg/m²) and overweight/obese (BMI ≥25 kg/m²) women show significantly elevated preterm birth risk, mediated through distinct biological mechanisms: underweight status impairs placental development through malnutrition and reduced leptin signaling, while obesity triggers chronic inflammation, insulin resistance, and oxidative stress that promote prostaglandin synthesis. ART patients face compounded risk factors including metabolic stress from superovulation, endometriosis and polycystic ovary syndrome comorbidities, and high-estrogenic states from ovulation induction that may alter BMI-related thresholds for adverse outcomes. Given that preterm birth is the leading cause of childhood mortality and morbidity including cerebral palsy, respiratory problems, and sensory impairments, optimizing pre-pregnancy BMI before ART procedures represents a critical intervention point for improving pregnancy outcomes.

title: Effects of Autoimmune Thyroiditis on Pregnancy Outcomes: Analysis of the Nationwide Inpatient Sample, 2016–2020
summary:
# Summary

This population-based analysis of over 500,000 pregnancies demonstrates that autoimmune thyroiditis significantly increases the risk of multiple adverse pregnancy outcomes, including gestational diabetes, preeclampsia, fetal growth restriction, abnormal fetal heart rate patterns, and labor complications such as induction failure. Thyroid autoantibodies, particularly anti-TPO and anti-thyroglobulin antibodies, drive these complications through multiple immune-mediated mechanisms including impaired trophoblast invasion, endothelial dysfunction, maternal immune dysregulation, and systemic inflammation. These pathophysiologic processes disrupt normal placental development and vascular function, leading to both maternal complications like preeclampsia and fetal complications including growth restriction and cardiac abnormalities. Notably, these adverse associations persist even in euthyroid individuals with thyroid autoimmunity, indicating that antibody presence rather than thyroid hormone levels alone accounts for the increased risk. The study reveals that associations are most pronounced in younger pregnant patients, suggesting age-dependent immune mechanisms. These findings underscore the clinical importance of identifying and potentially managing thyroid autoimmunity in pregnancy to mitigate serious obstetric complications.

title: Embryo Culture Duration Is an Independent Risk Factor for Gestational Diabetes in Frozen Embryo Transfer Pregnancies
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that prolonged embryo culture duration is an independent risk factor for gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) in frozen embryo transfer pregnancies, with day 6 blastocyst transfers showing the highest GDM incidence compared to day 3 and day 5 transfers. Blastocyst-stage transfers were associated with significantly higher GDM risk than cleavage-stage transfers, and this risk was amplified when combined with maternal metabolic risk factors including elevated BMI, advanced maternal age, elevated fasting glucose, and waist-to-hip ratio. The mechanism likely involves extended in vitro culture altering embryo-endometrial synchronization and affecting placental development and early fetal programming, with slower blastocyst development potentially impairing biochemical and epigenetic processes. While embryo morphological parameters alone were not associated with GDM incidence, the combination of blastocyst transfer with preexisting metabolic vulnerabilities substantially increased disease risk. These findings suggest that embryo culture protocols should be optimized and that cleavage-stage embryo transfer may reduce GDM risk in women with metabolic vulnerabilities, highlighting the critical role of in vitro culture conditions on both maternal metabolic outcomes and downstream pregnancy complications.

title: Perinatal complications and time to subsequent pregnancy after open, laparoscopic, and hysteroscopic myomectomy: a retrospective cohort study
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective cohort study evaluated perinatal complications and time to subsequent pregnancy across three myomectomy approaches (open, laparoscopic, and hysteroscopic) for uterine fibroid removal. Hysteroscopic myomectomy carried the highest risk of placenta accreta spectrum (5.2%) and gestational hypertension (17.5%), while laparoscopic myomectomy was uniquely associated with uterine rupture (1.0%), and open myomectomy had the highest rates of preterm delivery (5.0%) and cesarean delivery (90%). The risk of placental abnormalities following any myomectomy approach stems from impaired myometrial healing, uterine scar formation, and endometrial damage, with shorter intervals between surgery and subsequent pregnancy (<12 months) potentially increasing risks of both uterine rupture and placenta accreta spectrum. Notably, hysteroscopic myomectomy allowed the shortest time to subsequent pregnancy, suggesting a trade-off between faster fertility recovery and increased perinatal complications. These findings emphasize that perinatal risks after myomectomy must be individually assessed based on surgical approach and timing of subsequent pregnancy.

title: Association of maternal endometriosis with autism spectrum disorder in offspring: a retrospective cohort study
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective cohort study investigated the proposed association between maternal endometriosis and autism spectrum disorder (ASD) in offspring, hypothesizing that endometriosis-induced chronic inflammation could trigger adverse neurodevelopmental outcomes. Endometriosis, a chronic inflammatory pelvic disease, elevates interleukin-6 (IL-6) in both peritoneal fluid and serum, and during pregnancy IL-6 levels increase further, potentially activating maternal immune responses that cause fetal neuroinflammation through oxidative stress and mitochondrial dysfunction. While the inflammatory pathway from endometriosis through elevated IL-6 to ASD is theoretically plausible, the study's primary finding demonstrated no significant association between maternal endometriosis and ASD or ADHD in offspring after adjustment for baseline characteristics, despite endometriosis being linked to multiple obstetric complications including preeclampsia, preterm birth, placenta previa, and increased cesarean delivery. The results suggest that although endometriosis is associated with several adverse pregnancy outcomes, the direct causal link to autism spectrum disorder in offspring remains unsubstantiated, highlighting the complexity of maternal immune activation as a mechanism for neurodevelopmental disorders.

title: The type of endometrial preparation for embryo transfer after egg donation affects obstetric outcomes and the expression of placental angiogenic biomarkers
summary:
# Summary

This study investigated how endometrial preparation methods affect pregnancy outcomes in oocyte donation cycles by comparing natural/modified natural cycles with artificial cycles. Natural and modified natural cycles, which preserve corpus luteum function, significantly reduced hypertensive disorders of pregnancy, preeclampsia, placental previa, low birth weight, and neonatal intensive care unit admissions compared to artificial cycles. Mechanistically, natural cycles demonstrated elevated expression of pro-angiogenic factors (placental growth factor and vascular endothelial growth factor) that promote normal placental vascular development and spiral artery remodeling, whereas artificial cycles lacked corpus luteum production and showed elevated anti-angiogenic soluble Fms-Like Tyrosine Kinase-1 (sFlt-1), which inhibits these beneficial factors and causes endothelial dysfunction. The corpus luteum's production of relaxin and pregnancy-associated plasma protein-A (PAPP-A) appears critical for maternal cardiovascular and renal adaptation and placental development; their absence in artificial cycles contributes to adverse outcomes. These findings indicate that preserving the corpus luteum through natural cycle preparation significantly improves both placental angiogenic profiles and obstetric outcomes in oocyte donation pregnancies.

title: Association between endometriosis and adverse reproductive and perinatal outcomes in women undergoing assisted reproductive technology: a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review and meta-analysis demonstrates that endometriosis significantly compromises reproductive outcomes in women undergoing assisted reproductive technology. Women with endometriosis experience substantially reduced clinical pregnancy rates (relative risk 0.850) and live birth rates (relative risk 0.716) following ART procedures. Beyond fertility impacts, endometriosis is associated with multiple serious perinatal complications including increased risks of preterm birth (RR 1.277), placenta previa (RR 2.246), postpartum hemorrhage, cesarean delivery, low birth weight (RR 1.159), stillbirth, and hypertensive disorders of pregnancy (RR 1.161). The underlying mechanism likely involves chronic inflammation and endometrial dysfunction caused by ectopic endometrial tissue, which impairs placental development and uterine function. Notably, these adverse outcomes persist across all disease stages and show ethnic variation, with particularly elevated preterm birth risk in Asian populations (RR 1.626) and markedly increased placenta previa risk in Caucasian populations (RR 4.692). Despite ART's therapeutic benefit for endometriosis-related infertility, the condition itself fundamentally compromises pregnancy success and safety even after conception is achieved.

title: Female infertility and long-term cardiovascular risk: a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

This meta-analysis demonstrates that female infertility is associated with significantly elevated long-term cardiovascular risk, with infertile women showing higher incidence of coronary heart disease (HR=1.17) and cerebrovascular disease (HR=1.16) compared to fertile controls. Infertility may serve as a marker for cardiovascular disease through shared pathophysiological mechanisms including chronic inflammation and endothelial dysfunction. Exposure to assisted reproductive technology (ART) is similarly associated with increased overall cardiovascular risk (HR=1.17) and stroke risk (HR=1.12), suggesting that both the underlying infertility and its treatment modalities carry cardiovascular consequences. Underlying conditions linked to infertility, particularly polycystic ovary syndrome and metabolic syndrome, contribute through associated metabolic abnormalities including insulin resistance, dyslipidemia, and hypertension. Additionally, adverse pregnancy outcomes commonly observed in infertile women—including preeclampsia, gestational diabetes, and preterm delivery—are themselves independent markers of cardiometabolic risk. These findings highlight the importance of long-term cardiovascular surveillance in women with infertility and adverse reproductive outcomes.

title: Analysis of Risk Factors Affecting Cumulative Live Birth in Couples With Unexplained Infertility Undergoing IVF/ICSI
summary: This study identifies key clinical and biological factors affecting cumulative live birth rates in couples with unexplained infertility undergoing IVF/ICSI treatment. Female age ≥35 years and low anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH <1.2 ng/mL) emerge as the strongest negative predictors, reducing oocyte retrieval, embryo availability, and cumulative live birth rates through diminished ovarian reserve. Additional factors negatively impacting outcomes include advanced male age, elevated body mass index (≥27.5 kg/m²), reduced endometrial thickness (0.7–1.0 cm), elevated basal FSH, longer infertility duration, and elevated TSH levels. Conversely, higher antral follicle count and greater numbers of retrieved oocytes positively correlate with live birth success. Notably, the study reveals that patients with unexplained infertility have lower rates of adverse pregnancy outcomes (preterm birth 6.7%, gestational hypertension 4.7%, preeclampsia 0.9%) compared to those with other infertility diagnoses like PCOS, suggesting different underlying pathophysiology. The findings underscore that ovarian reserve markers and female age are the primary determinants of treatment success in this population, with embryo availability serving as a critical intermediate outcome linking these factors to cumulative live birth rates.

title: Vitamin D supplementation before in vitro fertilisation in women with polycystic ovary syndrome: multicentre, double blind, placebo controlled, randomised clinical trial
summary: This randomized controlled trial evaluated vitamin D supplementation (4000 IU/day for up to 90 days) in women with polycystic ovary syndrome undergoing in vitro fertilization, addressing the hypothesis that vitamin D deficiency—which is more prevalent in PCOS patients and associated with reduced ovulation and lower live birth rates—could be therapeutically targeted. Although vitamin D supplementation significantly elevated serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D levels compared to placebo, it did not improve live birth rates or other primary fertility outcomes including clinical pregnancy rates, ongoing pregnancy rates, or cumulative live birth rates at 6 and 12 months. Secondary analyses revealed no significant differences between groups in ovarian response outcomes, pregnancy complications (including miscarriage, gestational diabetes, and postpartum hemorrhage), or rates of severe ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome, and baseline vitamin D levels did not interact with treatment effect. These findings suggest that despite vitamin D receptors being expressed in reproductive tissues (ovary, endometrium, and placenta) where the vitamin is theoretically involved in folliculogenesis, endometrial receptivity, and implantation, supplementation at this dose does not translate biochemical improvements into clinical benefit for women with PCOS undergoing assisted reproduction.

title: ‘It Was a Bit of a Now or Never Situation’: Experiences of Preconception Care and Support for Women With Multiple Long‐Term Health Conditions
journal: Health Expectations : An International Journal of Public Participation in Health Care and Health Policy
summary:
# Summary

Women with multiple long-term health conditions face substantially elevated risks of adverse pregnancy outcomes, including maternal morbidity, preterm birth, low birth weight, and small for gestational age infants. Comprehensive preconception care—incorporating multidisciplinary medication management, condition-focused counseling, mental health support, and early specialist referrals—significantly reduces these risks and improves outcomes across miscarriage, congenital anomalies, and neonatal complications. However, women encounter substantial barriers to accessing adequate preconception care, including fragmented services, inconsistent clinical guidance, limited specialized availability, and gaps in professional knowledge, forcing many to self-advocate for information. Conditions such as endometriosis, cardiomyopathy, and Ehlers-Danlos syndrome require particularly tailored approaches, and progressive conditions often create psychological pressure for early pregnancy planning, while engagement with preconception care itself can generate emotional distress. Integration of services, early specialist input, and sensitive, realistic counseling are essential to optimize preconception support and empower women with complex medical needs to achieve successful pregnancies.

title: Coagulation parameter-based nomogram for the diagnosis of obstetric antiphospholipid syndrome and its subtypes
summary:
# Summary

Obstetric antiphospholipid syndrome (OAPS) is a maternal autoimmune condition driven by antiphospholipid antibodies that orchestrate vascular thrombosis and recurrent obstetric complications, including early pregnancy loss, fetal demise, and preterm delivery. The study identified multiple coagulation and immunologic parameters significantly dysregulated in OAPS patients compared to healthy controls, including elevated antinuclear antibody titers, anti-β2 glycoprotein I, thrombin-antithrombin complex, and Von Willebrand factor alongside reduced complement components (C3, C4) and anticoagulant factors (protein S, factor V). Through multivariate logistic regression analysis, antinuclear antibody, C3, anti-β2 glycoprotein I, thrombin-antithrombin complex, and Von Willebrand factor emerged as independent risk factors for OAPS diagnosis. The underlying pathophysiology involves complement system activation by antiphospholipid antibodies triggering thrombo-inflammation at the endothelial surface, combined with enhanced platelet aggregation and thrombin generation creating a prothrombotic state that compromises placental function and fetal outcomes. These coagulation parameter-based findings provide diagnostic and prognostic biomarkers for OAPS stratification and support the utility of antiplatelet and anticoagulation therapies in reducing obstetric morbidity and mortality.

title: Vitamin B12 and Reproductive Health: Clinical Insights, Emerging Mechanistic Understanding, and Nutritional Aspects
summary:
# Summary

Vitamin B12 deficiency impairs reproductive health through disruption of one-carbon metabolism and mitochondrial propionate metabolism, leading to elevated homocysteine and methylmalonic acid levels that promote oxidative stress and mitochondrial dysfunction. Epidemiological data from assisted reproductive technologies demonstrate that B12 deficiency is associated with shortened gestation, low birth weight, and reduced live birth rates, while elevated homocysteine in follicular fluid correlates with reduced oocyte maturation, poor embryo quality, and implantation failure. B12 acts as a coenzyme for methionine synthase and methylmalonyl-CoA mutase, and its antioxidant properties directly scavenge free radicals; deficiency consequently impairs gamete quality and fertility in both sexes. Elevated homocysteine is strongly associated with multiple gestational complications including preeclampsia, spontaneous abortion, placental abruption, and intrauterine growth restriction, while elevated methylmalonic acid impairs neural development and mitochondrial function by depleting intracellular glutathione and disrupting electron transport. Genetic polymorphisms in MTHFR (C677T and A1298C variants) that reduce enzyme activity increase susceptibility to repeated implantation failure by limiting folate availability for homocysteine remethylation, suggesting that adequate B12 and folate status optimizes reproductive outcomes in both natural conception and assisted reproduction.

title: Risk of miscarriage after benzodiazepine use during pregnancy: updated systematic review and meta-analysis
summary: This updated meta-analysis demonstrates that benzodiazepine exposure during early pregnancy is significantly associated with an increased risk of miscarriage, with pooled odds ratios for commonly prescribed agents including lorazepam, clonazepam, and alprazolam ranging from 1.42 to 1.82. Beyond miscarriage risk, benzodiazepine use during pregnancy correlates with multiple adverse birth outcomes including preterm birth, low birth weight, and neonatal intensive care unit admission. A dose-response relationship was identified across all three studies investigating this association, suggesting that increased benzodiazepine dosage amplifies miscarriage risk. The association persisted even after adjustment for publication bias, with an adjusted odds ratio of 1.58, indicating a robust finding rather than an artifact of reporting bias. Although benzodiazepines are therapeutically indicated for severe anxiety, agitation, epilepsy, and sleep disorders, these findings support current clinical guidelines from regulatory bodies such as NICE and the HAS that recommend against benzodiazepine use in pregnancy due to substantial fetal risks. The potential mechanisms underlying these adverse outcomes may involve benzodiazepine effects on cellular proliferation and differentiation processes critical to early fetal development.

title: Pregnancy and perinatal complications in untreated subfertile couples following singleton spontaneous conception: a narrative review
summary:
# Summary

This narrative review examines pregnancy and perinatal complications in untreated subfertile couples achieving spontaneous conception. Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) and endometriosis emerge as the primary reproductive disorders associated with multiple adverse outcomes, including gestational diabetes, hypertension, preeclampsia, and low birthweight. Untreated subfertile women demonstrate significantly increased risks for preterm birth, small-for-gestational-age infants, and low birthweight, with particularly elevated risks in women requiring ≥12 menstrual cycles to conceive, who also face increased placental disorders and postpartum hemorrhage. Endometriosis specifically links to inflammation, oxidative stress, genetic and epigenetic variations, and substantially elevates miscarriage, preeclampsia, and placenta previa risks. Children born to untreated subfertile women and non-treated PCOS women show heightened attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder symptoms and behavioral problems. While hormonal treatments (clomiphene citrate, letrozole, metformin, gonadotropins) effectively address anovulatory infertility and PCOS, hormonal therapy itself contributes to adverse pregnancy outcomes, necessitating careful treatment consideration for women seeking spontaneous conception.

title: Comparison of severe maternal morbidity between pregnancy with subclinical hypothyroidism and overt hypothyroidism
summary: Pregnancy with subclinical hypothyroidism confers a 54% increased risk of severe maternal morbidity at delivery compared with overt hypothyroidism, with particularly elevated rates of eclampsia occurring nearly three times more frequently. The condition is associated with multiple adverse pregnancy outcomes including preterm delivery, pregnancy loss, gestational diabetes, preeclampsia, placental abnormalities, and intrapartum complications such as obstructive labor and abnormal fetal heart rate patterns. Mechanistically, hypothyroidism may increase peripheral vascular resistance and trigger pregnancy-induced hypertension, while preeclampsia itself may bidirectionally induce hypothyroidism through elevated anti-angiogenic factors that impair nitric oxide production and capillary perfusion. Maternal demographic and comorbidity factors substantially modify risk, with younger maternal age, Black race, obesity, and pregestational hypertension each independently associated with higher severe morbidity rates in subclinical hypothyroidism. Patients with subclinical hypothyroidism also demonstrate higher prevalence of reproductive disorders including endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome, and uterine fibroids, suggesting a shared pathophysiologic basis. These findings underscore the clinical significance of detecting and potentially treating subclinical hypothyroidism in pregnancy, particularly among high-risk demographic groups.

title: An Unrecognized High Incidence of Asymptomatic Uterine Torsion in Pregnancies with Adenomyosis that Complicate Cesarean Delivery
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies a previously unrecognized association between focal-type adenomyosis and asymptomatic uterine torsion during pregnancy, occurring in 8.6% of cesarean deliveries in women with adenomyosis. All six cases of uterine torsion involved focal adenomyosis rather than diffuse disease, suggesting focal lesions may independently increase torsion risk by altering uterine morphology and reducing myometrial distensibility during pregnancy expansion. Uterine torsion substantially complicates cesarean delivery, forcing surgeons to employ atypical uterine incisions (83.3% of torsion cases) and supraumbilical skin incisions (50%) to navigate displaced anatomical structures, including the uterine vasculature and fallopian tubes. The critical challenge is that all torsion cases presented asymptomatically and were undetectable on routine antenatal ultrasound, rendering preoperative diagnosis impossible. The authors recommend tailored intraoperative strategies, including careful preoperative evaluation and use of intraoperative ultrasonography to identify optimal incision sites while avoiding adenomyotic lesions and placental tissue.

title: Effects of Age, Season, and Pregnancy History on Vitamin D Status in Women Seeking Preconception Care
journal: Medical Science Monitor: International Medical Journal of Experimental and Clinical Research
summary: Vitamin D plays multiple critical roles in female reproductive health, including regulation of follicular maturation, endometrial receptivity, and embryo implantation, while also supporting calcium homeostasis, immune function, and glucose metabolism. Vitamin D deficiency is significantly associated with endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome, impaired ovarian reserve, and multiple adverse pregnancy outcomes including hypertensive disorders, preterm birth, low birth weight, and miscarriage. This study examining women seeking preconception care found that serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D levels and vitamin D status are substantially influenced by age, pregnancy history, and season, with higher vitamin D concentrations and lower deficiency prevalence observed during summer and autumn months compared to winter and spring. Older women and those with prior pregnancy experience demonstrated higher circulating vitamin D levels and reduced deficiency rates, possibly reflecting greater health awareness and supplementation in these populations. These findings underscore the importance of assessing and optimizing vitamin D status in women planning pregnancy, particularly those living in regions with limited year-round sunlight exposure.

title: A Review of Traditional and Non-Traditional Cardiovascular Risk Factors in Women
summary: This review examines cardiovascular risk factors in women, with particular attention to traditional metabolic factors and female-specific conditions that influence long-term vascular health. Diabetes presents a disproportionately greater cardiovascular burden in women compared to men, increasing coronary heart disease mortality by 58%, while smoking similarly elevates coronary risk by 25% in women and additionally disrupts estrogen bioactivity and increases androgens, accelerating vascular damage. Body composition significantly modulates cardiovascular risk, with truncal and visceral fat deposition increasing risk while estrogen in premenopausal women promotes protective lower-body fat distribution; this protection reverses postmenopausally when estrogen withdrawal causes a sudden increase in cardiovascular risk. Pregnancy-related complications—including preeclampsia, gestational diabetes mellitus, and gestational hypertension—act as important cardiovascular risk predictors, with preeclampsia increasing long-term risks of coronary artery disease, heart failure, stroke, and hypertension, while gestational diabetes doubles future cardiovascular disease risk. Lipid metabolism varies across reproductive stages, with oral contraceptives modifying cholesterol profiles and menstrual cycle phases affecting LDL and HDL levels, yet familial hypercholesterolemia remains underdiagnosed and undertreated in women. Female-specific conditions including polycystic ovarian syndrome contribute to cardiovascular disease risk, emphasizing the importance of recognizing sex-specific biological pathways in cardiovascular prevention and management.

title: Cardiovascular risk prediction in women: rethinking traditional approaches through precision medicine
summary: This article examines cardiovascular risk prediction in women through a precision medicine lens, identifying multiple sex-specific pathways that elevate CVD risk beyond traditional male-derived models. Estrogen withdrawal during menopause triggers a cascade of adverse metabolic changes including increased visceral fat, impaired glucose tolerance, dyslipidemia, hypertension, endothelial dysfunction, and vascular inflammation, with early or surgical menopause accelerating loss of cardioprotection. Women-predominant autoimmune conditions such as systemic lupus erythematosus, rheumatoid arthritis, and Sjögren's syndrome drive CVD risk through chronic inflammation, endothelial dysfunction, and early atherosclerosis, while metabolic conditions like PCOS and smoking exposure confer disproportionately higher relative risk in women compared to men. Adverse pregnancy outcomes including pre-eclampsia, gestational hypertension, and gestational diabetes serve as predictive indicators of future CVD, and pregnancy-specific acute coronary syndrome risk is elevated by maternal age above 30 years, pre-eclampsia, coronary artery dissection, and gestational diabetes. The article advocates for incorporation of women-specific biomarkers such as high-sensitivity C-reactive protein, NT-proBNP, and lipoprotein(a) into refined risk stratification tools, moving beyond traditional scoring systems to enable precision-medicine approaches tailored to female cardiovascular physiology.

title: Uterine didelphys presenting as pelvic organ prolapse and second trimester uterine rupture: a case series and literature review
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
summary: Uterine didelphys, a rare Mullerian duct anomaly resulting from complete failure of ductal fusion, leads to duplication of uterine horns and cervices and is associated with multiple adverse reproductive and gynecologic outcomes. Women with this condition experience significantly elevated risks of recurrent miscarriage, recurrent pregnancy loss, preterm birth, malpresentations, obstructed labor, and uterine rupture, with the latter potentially occurring in the second trimester due to atypical myometrial architecture, reduced uterine distensibility, and poorly developed lower uterine segments. Endometriosis and infertility represent important long-term consequences, while approximately 40% of patients with didelphys variants demonstrate ipsilateral renal anomalies, necessitating comprehensive imaging evaluation. The case series presented here highlights the rare but serious complication of pelvic organ prolapse in association with uterine didelphys, a presentation previously documented in only two cases, occurring secondary to weakening and global dysfunction of pelvic supporting structures. The Herlyn-Werner-Wunderlich syndrome variant of uterine didelphys presents with the additional triad of obstructed hemivagina and ipsilateral renal agenesis. These findings underscore the importance of recognizing Mullerian duct anomalies early, particularly among women with infertility or recurrent adverse pregnancy outcomes, to enable appropriate management and counseling regarding reproductive risks.

title: Incidental findings of ipsilateral agenesis of the ovary and fallopian tube during cesarean section delivery successfully managed in a resource-limited setting: a case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
summary: This case report describes a rare combination of ipsilateral ovarian and fallopian tube agenesis discovered during cesarean delivery in a patient with an underlying unicornuate uterus, representing a complex Müllerian duct anomaly. Müllerian duct anomalies, which arise from defective development of the paramesonephric ducts and urogenital ridge during embryogenesis, are associated with significant reproductive complications including infertility, recurrent pregnancy loss, and endometriosis secondary to retrograde menstruation. The pathogenesis of adnexal agenesis may involve inadequate vascularization during early gestation, which disrupts the critical germ cell migration and autocrine/paracrine signaling required for proper ovarian development. Beyond reproductive dysfunction, this patient's case demonstrates the obstetric complications associated with Müllerian anomalies, including intrauterine growth restriction, abnormal fetal presentation, and placental abruption, necessitating cesarean delivery. The clinical presentation also highlights the frequent co-occurrence of renal anomalies with unilateral adnexal agenesis, underscoring the importance of comprehensive imaging evaluation. Successful management in a resource-limited setting emphasizes that careful clinical assessment and appropriate obstetric intervention can achieve favorable outcomes despite the complexity of these rare developmental anomalies.

title: Association between G6PD gene variants and adverse pregnancy outcomes
summary:
# Summary

This study examined the association between glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) gene variants and adverse pregnancy outcomes. G6PD, an X-chromosome-encoded enzyme essential for the pentose phosphate pathway and cellular energy metabolism, is frequently deficient in neonates carrying specific variants, particularly c.1376G > T and c.1388G > A, which account for nearly 74% of detected variants and correlate with markedly reduced enzyme activity. Carriers of G6PD variants experienced significantly elevated risks of preterm birth (13-fold increased odds) and neonatal jaundice (3-fold higher rate), with the severity of outcomes strongly correlating with enzyme activity grades. The study identified that pregnant women with G6PD variants combined with gestational diabetes mellitus faced substantially increased abortion risk, while comorbid cholestasis and cytopenia further elevated preterm birth risk. These findings underscore the critical role of G6PD in supporting endometrial decidualization and fertilized egg implantation, processes requiring intact pentose phosphate pathway function, and suggest that G6PD genotyping may help identify pregnancies at heightened risk for complications.

title: Advanced maternal age and assisted reproductive technologies: outcomes, genomics, and real-world evidence
summary: Advanced maternal age is associated with a broad spectrum of adverse pregnancy outcomes, including miscarriage, gestational diabetes, preeclampsia, placental complications, preterm birth, stillbirth, and neonatal intensive care admissions. The primary biological mechanism underlying these effects is progressive oocyte aneuploidy with advancing age, driven by deterioration in meiotic competence, mitochondrial dysfunction, and altered energy metabolism. Additionally, advanced maternal age triggers immune dysregulation characterized by reduced regulatory T cells, which normally regulate uterine natural killer cell function and decidual vascular remodeling, and contributes to maternal vascular dysfunction and endothelial dysfunction that impairs placental adaptation. Assisted reproductive technologies carry similar risks independent of maternal age, particularly for preeclampsia, placental abnormalities, and preterm birth, though donor oocytes mitigate miscarriage risk by reducing aneuploidy despite paradoxically elevating preeclampsia risk. Interventions including preimplantation genetic testing, low-dose aspirin prophylaxis, and oocyte cryopreservation for younger women planning delayed childbearing offer potential strategies to improve outcomes. The article emphasizes that identifying disrupted placental pathways specific to advanced maternal age or assisted reproductive technologies could enable development of precision therapies tailored to these populations.

title: Association between maternal risk factors and preterm birth in South Korea: a nationwide cohort study of 795,715 pregnancies
summary: This South Korean nationwide cohort study of 795,715 pregnancies identified multiple independent risk factors for preterm birth, which remains a leading cause of neonatal morbidity and mortality. Previous preterm birth emerged as the strongest predictor of recurrence (adjusted hazard ratio 4.05), followed by false labor (3.87), severe pregnancy complications including hypertension, preeclampsia, and placental abnormalities (2.21), and adolescent pregnancy (2.35). Additional significant risk factors included assisted reproductive technology conception (1.32), prior miscarriage (1.34), and lower socioeconomic status indicated by Medical Aid enrollment (1.65), highlighting important socioeconomic gradients in preterm birth risk. The study reveals that false labor, involving uterine contractions or premature cervical ripening, represents a proximate clinical warning sign rather than merely a risk marker. Endometriosis, along with other major complications such as placenta previa, placental abruption, and premature rupture of membranes, showed associations with increased preterm birth risk. These findings underscore the multifactorial nature of preterm birth pathogenesis and emphasize the importance of identifying pregnant women with high-risk profiles for enhanced clinical monitoring and intervention.

title: Genetically Causal Associations Between Adenomyosis/Endometriosis and Adverse Pregnancy Outcomes - A Two-Sample Mendelian Randomization Study
summary: This Mendelian randomization study investigated the causal relationships between adenomyosis/endometriosis and adverse pregnancy outcomes, leveraging genetic variants to establish causality beyond observational associations. The analysis revealed that endometriosis has a significant causal association with multiple consecutive miscarriage and recurrent pregnancy loss, with elevated CD200S expression on endometrial natural killer cells potentially mediating embryo rejection in affected women. However, contrary to observational data suggesting associations, the genetic analysis found no significant causal relationships between endometriosis and preterm birth, placental abruption, placenta previa, or low birth weight, nor between adenomyosis and most adverse outcomes. The study highlights important comorbidities including polycystic ovary syndrome, uterine fibroids, and uterine malformations that frequently coexist with these conditions and independently increase pregnancy complications, underscoring the need to account for multiple gynecological pathologies when assessing pregnancy risk. These findings suggest that while endometriosis causally contributes to pregnancy loss through immune dysregulation, many previously reported associations with other adverse outcomes may reflect confounding or reverse causation rather than direct causal mechanisms.

title: Severe maternal morbidity from conception to 6 weeks postpartum in Ontario: a population-based, longitudinal cohort study
summary:
# Summary

This population-based cohort study in Ontario identified severe maternal morbidity (SMM) rates and their temporal, demographic, and clinical associations across three pregnancy periods. Severe hemorrhage was the most common SMM type overall (6.10 per 1000 births), predominating during the intrapartum period, while sepsis was the leading postpartum complication (4.69 per 1000 births) and a major contributor to pregnancy-related maternal deaths. Type I diabetes emerged as the strongest predictor of antepartum SMM (odds ratio 6.19), largely through maternal ICU admissions and severe preeclampsia, while advanced maternal age (≥40 years) and prior SMM history were most strongly associated with intrapartum morbidity. Risk factors for SMM included sociodemographic vulnerabilities such as low income, rural residence, substance exposure, experience of assault, young age, and Black maternal race, alongside obstetric factors including nulliparity, multiple gestation, and operative delivery modes. The findings underscore distinct period-specific patterns of severe morbidity and identify high-risk populations where targeted preventive interventions could reduce maternal complications.

title: Weighted Cumulative Exposure Modelling to Assess the Association Between Reproductive Factors and Future Cardiovascular Disease in Women
summary: This study demonstrates that severe maternal morbidity (SMM) during pregnancy and childbirth—including preeclampsia, obstetric hemorrhage, pregnancy-associated stroke, and renal impairment—significantly increases women's long-term cardiovascular disease risk through vascular endothelial dysfunction and subclinical vascular impairment.
