Beyond acute pregnancy complications, reproductive conditions including endometriosis, infertility, and polycystic ovarian syndrome are independently associated with both poor metabolic health and premature cardiovascular disease. The research quantifies this risk, showing that non-hypertensive cardiac SMM carries the highest cardiovascular risk (9.11 per 1000 person-years), followed by hypertensive SMM (4.55 per 1000 person-years), while hemorrhagic SMM accounts for the highest proportion of overall SMM cases. These findings establish reproductive health events as important predictors of future cardiovascular disease in women, highlighting the need for integrated cardiovascular surveillance in women with histories of pregnancy complications or chronic reproductive conditions.

title: Anti-β2GPI/HLA-DR Antibody, Chronic Endometritis, and Uterine Endometrial Microbiome in Women with Recurrent Pregnancy Loss: A Prospective Cohort Study
summary:
# Summary

This prospective cohort study identifies three interconnected pathogenic mechanisms contributing to recurrent pregnancy loss: anti-β2GPI/HLA-DR antibody positivity, chronic endometritis, and endometrial dysbiosis. Anti-β2GPI/HLA-DR antibodies target antigens expressed on decidual endothelial cells and disrupt placental function, while chronic endometritis—indicated by elevated CD138-positive plasma cells—promotes a pro-inflammatory uterine environment. Endometrial dysbiosis, characterized by non-Lactobacillus-dominant microbiota (particularly with increased Gardnerella and Ureaplasma species), further compromises pregnancy viability by reducing beneficial Lactobacillus populations, especially the protective Lactobacillus crispatus. A multimodal treatment approach addresses each pathology: low-dose aspirin and heparin for antibody positivity, antibiotics (metronidazole or doxycycline) for chronic endometritis with targeted agents for Ureaplasma, and probiotics for dysbiosis restoration. Integration of these three diagnostic assessments reduced unexplained recurrent pregnancy loss cases from 65.5% to 16.1%, substantially improving diagnostic accuracy and enabling personalized therapeutic interventions that achieved live birth rates exceeding 90% in treated cohorts.

title: Screening-detected refractive errors in infants born after assisted reproductive technology: a prospective cohort study
summary: This prospective cohort study examined refractive errors in infants born after assisted reproductive technology (ART) compared to spontaneously conceived controls, finding a higher prevalence of screening-detected refractive errors in ART-conceived infants (11.4% versus lower rates in naturally conceived infants). The study revealed that specific ART protocols differentially influence refractive development, with infants conceived using GnRH-antagonist protocols showing significantly reduced risk of refractive errors compared to other stimulation approaches, while the microstimulation protocol also conferred modest protective effects. Maternal infertility diagnoses including endometriosis, pelvic or tubal factors, ovarian dysfunction, and thyroid disorders were all associated with refractive errors in offspring, suggesting that underlying reproductive pathology may influence fetal visual development independent of the ART procedure itself. ART-conceived twins exhibited higher prevalence of refractive errors than singletons, and pregnancies complicated by preterm birth, low birth weight, gestational diabetes, and hypertensive disorders showed associations with visual developmental outcomes. Notably, specific ART technical parameters such as embryo quality, preimplantation genetic diagnosis, embryo transfer stage, and number of embryos transferred did not significantly influence refractive error risk, indicating that the protocol choice rather than procedural specifics may be the critical determinant. These findings suggest that both the underlying maternal infertility condition and the hormonal stimulation protocol employed during ART may impact fetal ocular development and subsequent refractive outcomes in early infancy.

title: Predicting failed trial of labor in advanced-age primiparas: development and validation of a clinical nomogram
summary:
# Article Summary: Predicting Failed Trial of Labor in Advanced-Age Primiparas

Advanced maternal age (≥35 years) substantially increases the risk of failed trial of labor (TOL) and associated maternal and neonatal complications in first-time mothers. A multivariable analysis identified multiple independent risk factors for TOL failure, including greater gestational weight gain, higher pre-pregnancy BMI, increased gestational age, estimated fetal weight ≥4.0 kg, maternal anemia, abnormal amniotic fluid volume, premature rupture of membranes, hypertensive disorders of pregnancy, and labor induction. Conversely, taller maternal height and epidural analgesia emerged as protective factors associated with successful TOL. The study proposes that prioritizing gestational weight control, anemia correction, and epidural analgesia utilization could improve vaginal delivery rates while reducing the adverse sequelae associated with cesarean delivery, including uterine rupture, placental complications, and iatrogenic endometriosis. Failed TOL subjects mothers to considerable physical and psychological stress and places neonates at increased risk of adverse outcomes compared to successful vaginal delivery, which is associated with lower postpartum blood loss and improved neonatal outcomes.

title: Off to a good start: current gaps and priorities in early-life microbiome research
summary:
# Summary

The microbiome serves as a powerful regulator of human health and disease, with maternal microbes influencing fetal development and subsequent generations already during gestation. During pregnancy, the gut microbiome performs critical functions including supporting pregnancy physiology, promoting fetal development, and enabling microbial transmission to the newborn. The infant microbiome composition is shaped by multiple perinatal factors including delivery mode, maternal microbiota, maternal diet, peripartum antibiotic exposure, and early feeding practices, with gestational age and delivery method strongly associated with both microbiome composition and immune development. Both preterm birth and cesarean delivery are consistently linked to altered microbial colonization patterns, including delayed establishment of obligate anaerobes, disrupted bifidobacterial succession, and increased abundance of facultative anaerobes and opportunistic taxa. Early-life microbiome perturbations caused by factors such as antibiotic exposure and delivery complications are associated with increased risk of infectious diseases, non-communicable diseases, and early-life inflammatory conditions through altered signaling between microbiota and host. These findings underscore critical gaps in understanding how early microbiome development relates to long-term health outcomes, including conditions relevant to reproductive health.

title: Investigating Outcomes of Gamete Donation in Assisted Reproductive Technology: A Retrospective Study
summary: This retrospective study of gamete donation outcomes in assisted reproductive technology reveals significant associations between treatment modality and adverse pregnancy outcomes. Gamete donation effectively treats infertility caused by various factors, with oocyte donation addressing Premature Ovarian Insufficiency and sperm donation treating azoospermia, which accounted for nearly 80% of male factor cases. However, ART procedures are associated with substantial risks including preterm birth, cesarean delivery, low birth weight, and multiple gestation, with these complications varying by donation type—oocyte donation cases showed 40% preterm delivery rates and 43.4% low birth weight incidence, while sperm donation cases demonstrated 29.4% preterm deliveries. Single embryo transfer effectively reduces multiple gestations compared to two-embryo transfer, which significantly increased multiple pregnancy occurrence and associated adverse outcomes. Notably, frozen embryo transfer cycles showed higher preterm birth rates (40%) compared to fresh cycles (29.8%), and maternal age over 37 years was associated with predominantly non-ongoing pregnancies, highlighting the importance of treatment selection based on individual patient factors in optimizing ART outcomes.

title: Racialized economic segregation in relation to fecundability in a preconception cohort study
journal: Journal of urban health : bulletin of the New York Academy of Medicine
summary:
# Summary

This preconception cohort study demonstrates that racialized economic segregation significantly reduces fecundability, with individuals living in the most disadvantaged neighborhoods showing moderately reduced fertility compared to those in privileged areas. The associations are primarily driven by income-based segregation rather than racial composition alone, as evidenced by strong monotonic inverse associations with the Index of Concentration at the Extremes (ICE) income metric. The study proposes that segregation becomes biologically embedded through oxidative stress and systemic inflammation, pathways that independently associate with reduced fertility and align with established literature linking perceived stress and socioeconomic disadvantage to impaired reproductive outcomes. Beyond fertility, racialized economic segregation was associated with multiple adverse pregnancy and birth outcomes including hypertensive disorders, preterm birth, low birth weight, and stillbirth. The researchers also highlight environmental mediators—including redlining, green space availability, air pollution exposure, proximity to major roads, and discrimination—as potential mechanisms linking neighborhood disadvantage to impaired fertility outcomes. These findings underscore how structural inequities embedded in residential geography translate into measurable reproductive health disparities.

title: Epigenetic alterations in preeclampsia: a systematic review of current mechanisms and biomarker potential
summary:
# Summary

Preeclampsia, a major cause of maternal and fetal morbidity worldwide, involves multiple pathogenic mechanisms centered on placental dysfunction and angiogenic imbalance. This systematic review identifies epigenetic dysregulation—encompassing DNA methylation, histone modifications, and non-coding RNAs—as key contributors to preeclampsia pathogenesis. Aberrant DNA methylation patterns disrupt genes regulating angiogenic factors including vascular endothelial growth factor, soluble fms-like tyrosine kinase-1, and placental growth factor, while dysregulated microRNAs and long non-coding RNAs modulate endothelial activation, oxidative stress, and inflammatory responses. Histone modifications alter chromatin accessibility in trophoblast cells, impairing migration and invasion, while hypoxia-induced epigenetic changes amplify trophoblastic stress and exacerbate endothelial dysfunction. Emerging epigenetic biomarkers—including circulating cell-free DNA methylation patterns and non-coding RNA signatures—show promise for early prediction and stratification of preeclampsia severity, with environmental factors such as obesity and dietary patterns influencing maternal epigenetic landscapes and disease susceptibility.

title: The Association Between Diabetes Mellitus During Pregnancy and Retinopathy of Prematurity
summary: This study examined whether maternal diabetes mellitus during pregnancy increases retinopathy of prematurity (ROP) risk in preterm infants, with particular attention to underlying vascular mechanisms. The mechanistic pathway involves maternal hyperglycemia upregulating vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) expression and inducing oxidative stress-mediated endothelial dysfunction, which disrupts normal retinal vascularization. Additionally, disrupted insulin signaling in the hyperglycemic maternal environment suppresses insulin-like growth factor-1 (IGF-1) levels, and reduced IGF-1 further impairs VEGF-dependent vessel development in the immature retina. However, the study's primary finding was that no statistically significant association was observed between maternal diabetes and ROP development, contrary to theoretical predictions based on metabolic mechanisms. Established ROP risk factors remained dominant, including lower gestational age, lower birthweight, prolonged oxygen supplementation, and advanced maternal age, while prenatal corticosteroid treatment emerged as a significant protective factor. These findings suggest that although maternal hyperglycemia can theoretically compromise fetal retinal vascular development through multiple pathways, the prematurity-related factors and neonatal oxygen exposure exert stronger influences on ROP development than maternal glycemic status alone.

title: Association between adenomyosis and maternal and neonatal outcomes: a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary: This meta-analysis demonstrates that adenomyosis, characterized by abnormal presence of endometrial glands and stroma within the myometrium, is significantly associated with multiple adverse maternal and neonatal outcomes. Women with adenomyosis exhibit substantially reduced clinical pregnancy rates (RR 0.753) and live birth rates (RR 0.613), alongside markedly elevated risks of miscarriage, preterm birth, placental complications (including placenta previa), pre-eclampsia, hypertensive disorders, postpartum hemorrhage, and neonatal complications such as low birth weight and small for gestational age. The adverse effects of adenomyosis persist even in women undergoing assisted reproductive technology, who continue to experience diminished pregnancy success and increased complications including miscarriage and pre-eclampsia. Notably, ethnicity-based subgroup analyses revealed that Asian populations are particularly susceptible to these adenomyosis-associated complications, with significantly higher risks observed across multiple outcomes compared to Caucasian populations. These findings establish adenomyosis as a significant clinical risk factor for adverse perinatal outcomes and highlight the importance of targeted management strategies, particularly in high-risk populations.
